win.reason: Confirmed Swiss XI is the stronger, more cohesive side — Kobel behind an Akanji/Elvedi/Rodriguez spine, Xhaka-Freuler-Sow controlling midfield. Switzerland need a win to top Group B, so they will press; Canada (Crepeau in goal, Davies on the bench recovering from a hamstring) advance with a draw and may sit deeper and counter through David/Buchanan. Market has SUI ~+135, draw ~+200, CAN ~+230 — no certainty. Both already qualified and rotating, so a well-matched cagey game is a real outcome; DNB protects against the live draw risk rather than chasing the straight win.

scorers.note: Leads the line in the confirmed XI; 2 WC goals (one penalty vs Qatar, equaliser vs Bosnia), 25 international goals. In form but a 29-year-old target striker against an organised Cornelius/De Fougerolles pairing — a threat, NOT a banker.

Canada's clear focal point and penalty taker; hat-trick vs Qatar after a quiet run. Highest-floor scorer on the pitch given he both takes and wins spot-kicks, but Akanji/Elvedi limit open-play chances.
Confirmed starter (replaced Ndoye), scored vs Bosnia; direct wide threat with free-kick/corner duty. Real secondary goal route from out-to-in cuts against Laryea.
20-year-old making his first WC start after a two-goal cameo off the bench vs Bosnia; flexible attacking-mid/wide role. Live wildcard scorer if Switzerland chase the win.

assists.note: Deep-lying creator and the side's primary penalty taker; dictates Swiss build-up and dead-ball delivery, prime assist source for Embolo/Vargas.
Corner and crossing duty plus 1v1 wide creation; chief open-play supply line for Embolo at the back post.
Canada's most direct wide creator, helped engineer Larin's opener vs Qatar; main assist outlet feeding David on the counter.

penalty.note: Both sides carry strong spot-kick takers. Xhaka is Switzerland's confirmed first choice (converted vs Bosnia and vs Jordan), Embolo next. For Canada, Jonathan David is automatic — most club penalties in the squad and the player most often fouled in the box; Larin (six club pens) deputises if David is off.

lineups.home: Gregor Kobel, Manuel Akanji, Nico Elvedi, Ricardo Rodríguez, Luca Jaquez, Johan Manzambi, Granit Xhaka, Remo Freuler, Breel Embolo, Rubén Vargas, Djibril Sow
lineups.away: Maxime Crépeau, Derek Cornelius, Luc de Fougerolles, Richie Laryea, Alistair Johnston, Mathieu Choinière, Nathan Saliba, Ali Ahmed, Tajon Buchanan, Cyle Larin, Jonathan David

playerNotes.note: KEY BENCH — not starting, recovering from a hamstring injury; Marsch confirmed he will not start but is available as an impact substitute. A second-half introduction sharply raises Canada's threat down the left.
KEY BENCH — dropped from the XI (Vargas/Manzambi preferred). A live-game changer off the bench; do NOT treat as a starter for scorer markets, but a real late entrant if Switzerland chase a winner.
Captain, midfield metronome and first-choice penalty taker; controls tempo and dead-ball delivery — central to both creation and any spot-kick.
Anchors the back four alongside Elvedi; pace and reading key to nullifying David/Buchanan in transition. Switzerland's clean-sheet hopes run through him.
Starts in goal for Canada; busy night likely if Switzerland dominate possession — his shot-stopping is central to any Canada under/draw outcome.
Canada's all-time top scorer, starts alongside David; scored in back-to-back games (Bosnia, Qatar). Secondary scorer and penalty backup, dangerous on crosses and second balls.

win.reason: Bosnia's deeper, more proven side (Dzeko, Demirovic, Kolasinac, Bundesliga-based core) faces a Qatar XI shattered 6-0 by Canada and missing Almoez Ali up top - Edmilson Junior starts but has 0 goals in 15 caps. Both must win to chase a best-third place, so Bosnia push, but they were poor in the 4-1 Switzerland loss and both defences leak, so this is a ~55% call, not a lock - a draw sends both home and Qatar are desperate.

scorers.note: Bosnia's primary striker and first-choice penalty taker (12 Bundesliga goals for Stuttgart 25/26); led the line and carries the goal burden, but missed a spot-kick vs Wales in the playoff and has just 1 goal in 7 WC-cycle games.
Captain and all-time top scorer but 40 years old - a poacher's threat in the box, NOT a banker; minutes likely managed, started alongside Demirovic.

Qatar's chief creator, penalty taker and standout (40 goals/127 caps, Asian Cup top scorer with a hat-trick of penalties in the 2023 final). Best route to a Qatar goal, especially from the spot against a leaky Bosnia back line.
PSV winger who scored the winning shoot-out penalty vs Italy to send Bosnia through; pace and final ball from the flank, live for a goal or assist.

assists.note: Qatar's chief creator - dribbling and final ball are their best supply line.
Bosnia's wing creativity; deliveries and cut-backs to Demirovic/Dzeko.
Attacking left-back, overlaps and crosses into the box for the strikers.

penalty.backup: Edin Dzeko (Bosnia); Edmilson Junior / Hassan Alhaydos (Qatar)
penalty.note: Both defences are leaky and both sides are desperate for a win, raising box contact and spot-kick chances. Afif is a near-automatic, ice-cold penalty taker (penalty hat-trick in the 2023 Asian Cup final); Demirovic is Bosnia's No.1 taker but missed at the goalkeeper vs Wales in the playoff - reason to be cautious on a Bosnia pen converting.

playerNotes.note: BENCHED - Qatar's all-time top scorer is NOT in the confirmed XI; attack is Edmilson, Afif, Alhaydos. He is the key bench option / likely game-changer if Qatar chase, so live as a substitute scorer, not a starter pick.
Penalty taker and chief creator (40 goals/127 caps). Qatar's single biggest threat - their best chance of a goal runs through him, especially from the spot.
Starts on the wing but has 0 goals in 15 international caps - creativity over end product; more an assist/chance-creation threat than a scorer.
First-choice penalty taker and main striker (12 Bundesliga goals 25/26) but missed a spot-kick vs Wales in the playoff and has only 1 goal in 7 WC-cycle matches - quietly off-form internationally.
Captain, 40 years old, all-time top scorer. A box presence and leader but NOT a banker scorer; minutes may be managed.
Homam Ahmed and Assim Madibo suspended; Qatar also picked up two red cards in the 6-0 loss to Canada - thin, disrupted spine adds to their fragility.

win.reason: Brazil field a near-full-strength front line (Vinicius, Paqueta, Cunha, Rayan) and top the group, but with a knockout spot already secured Ancelotti has flagged a measured rather than statement performance, and Scotland defend deep with progression on the line. ~62% implied win prob, not certainty.

scorers.note: Leads the line after a record-equalling first-half brace vs Haiti; in form and assured of minutes with Raphinha out, but not a guaranteed banker against a deep-sitting Scotland block.
Involved in 6 goals in 6 WC appearances (3G 3A); pace mismatch on Patterson on the left, sharpened by Miami heat. Brazil's likeliest scorer.
Now Brazil's primary penalty and free-kick taker with Raphinha out; arrives in the box from the No.10 role, adding a penalty/set-piece scoring route.
Scotland's chief goal threat via late runs into the box and set-pieces from Robertson delivery; their realistic route to the goal/nick they may need.

assists.note: Scotland's main creative outlet from left-back; dangerous set-piece and crossing delivery is their primary supply line to McTominay/Shankland.
Central creator linking Vinicius and Cunha and on free-kicks/corners now Raphinha is absent.
Three assists already this tournament; his left-side carries set up chances for Cunha and Rayan arriving.

penalty.note: With Raphinha (first-choice) out, Paqueta is Brazil's primary penalty taker; Neymar, available off the bench per Ancelotti, would likely assume duties if brought on. Scotland defending deep makes a Brazil spot-kick plausible but not high-likelihood.

lineups.home: Angus Gunn, Scott McKenna, Jack Hendry, Andy Robertson, Nathan Patterson, Scott McTominay, Kenny McLean, Lewis Ferguson, Lawrence Shankland, John McGinn, Ben Gannon-Doak

playerNotes.note: Starts up top over the projected Che Adams; a penalty-box finisher who started vs Haiti, sat out Morocco, and is recalled to give Scotland a recognised in-area threat against Brazil.
Comes into midfield ahead of Christie, sitting alongside Ferguson and McTominay in a 4-3-3 built to defend deep and spring McTominay's late runs.
Passed fit after a calf injury and partners Hendry at centre-back; key to Scotland's low block holding against Vinicius and Cunha.
19-year-old Bournemouth winger starts on the right in place of the hamstrung Raphinha after a solid cameo vs Haiti; raw but direct, a relative unknown for Patterson/Robertson's side.
Brazil's player of the tournament (3G 3A in 6 WC apps); pace threat down the left directly against Patterson, exacerbated by Miami heat.
Leads the line after a first-half brace vs Haiti, keeping Igor Thiago on the bench; Brazil's in-form No.9 but facing a packed Scottish box.

win.reason: Morocco field a full-strength XI (Ouahbi confirmed no rotation, targeting top spot on GD over Brazil) with El Kaabi now a true #9 against an eliminated Haiti sitting in a deep block. But Morocco have scored only once per game (1-1 Brazil, 1-0 Scotland) and Haiti conceded just one to Scotland, so a low-scoring Atlas Lions win is likeliest rather than a romp; the deep-block-vs-possession profile keeps a real draw/upset tail (~35-40%).

scorers.note: Morocco's all-time-form striker (35 goals/71 caps; 18+ league goals this season) starts as the recognised #9 after Saibari's false-9 role; raises his scoring odds sharply, but a packed Haiti box and Morocco's one-goal-a-game output mean he is NOT a guaranteed banker.
Scored both Morocco goals so far (vs Brazil and the fastest goal of the tournament vs Scotland); now pushed to attacking mid behind El Kaabi, still arriving in the box and in red-hot form.
Assisted both Morocco goals and was AFCON top scorer (5 in 5); shares penalty duty with Hakimi, so a spot-kick lifts his scorer chance against a side that may concede a pen under sustained pressure.
Captain on his 99th cap, Morocco's main outlet bombing from RB and a penalty/free-kick option; threat from range and from the spot against a deep block, though primarily a creator here.

assists.note: Two assists in two games (both for Saibari); chief creator unpicking a low block, prime assist candidate again.
Primary corner/free-kick taker plus overlapping crosses from RB; set-piece delivery is a strong assist route versus a packed box.
Creative left-side No.10 and set-piece specialist; already teed up chances (squared for Saibari's crossbar effort), live for an assist breaking a deep block.

penalty.note: Morocco's sustained pressure on a deep, sometimes-naive Haiti back line (offside-trap holes vs Brazil) makes a penalty plausible; Hakimi (captain) and Brahim Diaz are the listed primary takers, with Soufiane Rahimi a further option off the bench.

playerNotes.note: Now starts as the recognised #9 (Saibari was the false-9 earlier); Morocco's leading scorer with 35 goals in 71 caps and 18+ club goals this season, giving the Atlas Lions a genuine target man against the box.
Shifts from false-9 to attacking midfield behind El Kaabi; has scored both of Morocco's goals at the tournament, including its fastest-ever World Cup goal vs Scotland.
Comes in to anchor the double pivot alongside El Aynaoui, screening for an expected Haiti counter through Isidor's pace.
Captain making his 99th cap; primary set-piece and joint-penalty taker and Morocco's chief attacking outlet from right-back; Anass Salah-Eddine starts at LB in place of Mazraoui.
BENCHED despite being Haiti's most prolific attacker (34 goals in 51 caps) and main 6ft4 aerial threat; available as an impact substitute, with Lenny Joseph preferred from the start.
Sunderland striker leading the line for an eliminated Haiti who will sit in Migne's deep block; their main counter-attacking outlet via pace, still searching for his first tournament goal.

win.reason: Mexico already qualified as Group A winners and have rotated heavily (Rangel for Ochoa, G. Martinez leading instead of Jimenez/Gimenez, Fidalgo out), so the edge is softer than the bare names suggest. Czechia are do-or-die and MUST win, so they will chase rather than sit deep — that creates a genuine draw/Czechia-goal pathway and downgrades any comfortable Mexico win-to-nil. Home advantage at altitude plus the stronger spine (E. Alvarez, Montes, Mora, Quinones) still tips it Mexico's way, but ~50-53% only.

scorers.note: Mexico's most reliable in-form attacker and an undisputed starter under Aguirre; sharpest goal threat in a rotated front line.
Leads the Czech line with Schick benched; had a close-range chance saved vs South Korea and is also on free-kick duty.
Handed the start as Mexico's No.9 ahead of Jimenez/Gimenez; central striker so gets the best chances but unproven at this level — not a banker.
Czech captain and primary aerial set-piece threat — scored a header off a Coufal delivery vs South Korea; Czechia's set-piece route to a goal.

assists.note: Czechia's primary corner and free-kick taker; assist threat comes from his deliveries (and long throws) onto Krejci/Hlozek.
17-year-old creative starter on Mexico set pieces; main creator from open play and dead balls.
Wide creator feeding the Mexican strikers; on the corner/free-kick rota too.

penalty.note: Czechia's designated takers Schick (1st) and Soucek (2nd) are BOTH benched, so among starters free-kick takers Sulc and Hlozek become the de-facto penalty options. For Mexico (Jimenez benched), Alvarado or teenager Mora would step up. With both sides rotated and Czechia chasing, a spot-kick is a live but not high-probability scenario.

playerNotes.note: Benched after going scoreless in the first two games; Czechia's first-choice penalty taker and top scorer is only a bench option — a likely impact sub if they need a goal, but NOT a banker starting.
Promoted to lead the line in Schick's place; also on free-kick/penalty duty among the starters. Hoffenheim forward, part of the Hoffenheim trio starting.
Captain and aerial set-piece weapon — already scored a header off a Coufal long throw vs South Korea; Czechia's best route to a goal in a must-win game.
Starts in goal as Aguirre rotates Ochoa out; Mexico have already locked top spot so the keeper change carries low stakes.
Surprise No.9 start ahead of Jimenez and Gimenez (both benched); leads a rotated front line — central chances but unproven, so a speculative rather than banker scorer.
17-year-old retains his starting role — Mexico's youngest-ever WC starter; chief creator and on the set-piece rota despite his age.

win.reason: Korea (3-4-2-1) only need a draw to top the group and have rested Son Heung-min, Castrop and Cho Gue-sung — a deliberately cautious, rotated side content to control and sit. South Africa MUST win and will chase, but they are without suspended Teboho Mokoena (their penalty taker and midfield screen) and start Makgopa over Foster. A possession-content favourite vs a must-win underdog forced to open up is a classic stalemate/low-event profile; ~55% is not a Korea banker, so the draw and double-chance carry real value.

scorers.note: Confirmed Korea lone striker in the 3-4-2-1; gets the central chances but in a side set up to manage the game, service may be limited
Starts as a No.10/second line; pace in behind a chasing SA defence on the counter is Korea's likeliest goal route
Korea's chief set-piece and penalty taker; direct free-kick and spot-kick threat, plus arrives late from the second line
Confirmed SA starting striker ahead of Foster; SA must win so they'll commit bodies forward, but breaking down a back three is hard

assists.note: Tournament's most accurate distributor; Korea's primary creator and dead-ball deliverer from the second line
SA's left-side creator/pace; their main chance-creation outlet with Mokoena out
Confirmed SA starter after impressing off the bench vs Czechia; youthful creativity feeding Makgopa

penalty.taker: Lee Kang-in (South Korea); Oswin Appollis (South Africa, primary now Mokoena is suspended)
penalty.backup: Lyle Foster (SA, off the bench); Hwang In-beom (Korea)
penalty.note: SA lose their proven spot-kick taker Mokoena (scored the pen vs Czechia) to suspension — penalty duty is less settled and falls to Appollis or Foster once on. Korea's Lee Kang-in is a calm designated taker. A chasing SA side drawing fouls in the box is the main penalty scenario.

playerNotes.note: BENCHED — not in the starting XI. Hong Myung-bo's bold call with only a draw needed. Likely 2nd-half impact sub and a genuine late scorer/assist threat if Korea need to chase or kill the game — a sub option, NOT a starting-XI scorer pick.
Confirmed lone striker in place of Son; carries Korea's central goal threat but in a side built to manage and protect a draw.
Confirmed starter and Korea's key creator/set-piece + penalty taker; the most likely source of a Korea goal via assist or dead ball.
SUSPENDED (yellow accumulation) — OUT. Removes SA's penalty taker and midfield screen; Sithole comes in to replace him.
Confirmed to lead the line ahead of Lyle Foster, who starts on the bench as an impact sub — a notable selection given SA must win.
On the BENCH despite being fit; an impact-sub scorer/penalty option if introduced as SA chase the win.

win.reason: Türkiye led the tournament for shot volume (62 shots, 0 goals in two games — most shots without a goal at a WC since 1966) and face a USA side making a record nine changes with Pulisic, Adams, Balogun, Richards and A. Robinson all off. Possession-heavy hosts-style vs a rotated, de-prioritised opponent is a regression-up spot — but the same wastefulness that eliminated them keeps this only a slight edge, not a lock; the draw stays genuinely live.

scorers.note: Pushed up to lead the line with Çalhanoğlu benched; Türkiye's most dynamic forward, primary goal threat against a makeshift back four (Trusty/M.Robinson/McKenzie). Not a banker — Türkiye's finishing has been abject (0 goals on 62 shots).
Operating in the No.10 underneath Yıldız and now Türkiye's de-facto set-piece/penalty taker with Çalhanoğlu and Aktürkoğlu both out — live for a free-kick or spot-kick goal as well as open play.
USA lone striker, penalty-box poacher; scored vs Australia and is the focal point with Balogun rested and Pulisic benched. Service may be thin given heavy rotation around him.
Wide attacker with direct running and shot volume; gets into scoring positions repeatedly in this shot-heavy Türkiye setup.

assists.note: Chief creator and set-piece deliverer for Türkiye; corners and free-kicks now run through him with Çalhanoğlu benched — leading assist source.
Deep-lying half of the pivot, progressive passer who can pick out the front line and steps up on some set-piece delivery.
Most senior USA midfielder left after the rotation; box-arriving runner and the likeliest creator/supplier for Pepi.

penalty.backup: Kenan Yıldız / Orkun Kökçü (TUR); Ricardo Pepi or Weston McKennie (USA)
penalty.note: With Çalhanoğlu and Aktürkoğlu both off the pitch, Güler inherits Türkiye's penalty/free-kick duty; Yıldız and Kökçü are alternates. For USA, with Pulisic benched and Balogun rested, the spot-kick would fall to Pepi or McKennie. Türkiye's possession dominance makes them the more likely side to win one.

Drops into the playmaking No.10 underneath Yıldız and becomes the team's main set-piece/penalty taker; the single most important creative node in this XI.
Dropped to the bench despite being first-choice pivot and set-piece taker — a major game-changer if introduced; removes a proven penalty/free-kick specialist from the starting XI.
Retains the lone-striker role with Balogun rested (yellow) and Pulisic benched; a poacher who scored vs Australia but may be starved of service amid the nine changes.
On the bench — fit-again after a calf issue but unlikely to manage 90 minutes; the obvious second-half game-changer USA hold in reserve since the group is already won.
Part of a heavily reshuffled back four (with Trusty and McKenzie) after Richards and A. Robinson were rested — an untested, makeshift defensive unit Türkiye's shot volume can target.

win.reason: Opta had it near-even with a real draw branch. Paraguay must win and will dominate the ball, but they attack a deep, organised Australia block (28.3% possession + clean sheet vs Türkiye) that needs only a draw to go through and is content to sit, frustrate and counter. Losing Almirón (top scorer, prime creator and dead-ball taker) blunts Paraguay's edge against a packed box. ~38% Paraguay, with the draw genuinely live — back Paraguay on Draw-No-Bet rather than a clean win.

scorers.note: Leads the line (in for Sanabria) but a 34-yo veteran target man at Independiente vs a Souttar–Circati centre-back pairing — service is thin now Almirón's set-piece delivery is gone. Non-banker.
Paraguay's focal creator and now primary penalty/free-kick taker with Almirón out; their likeliest goal source from open play or a dead ball.
Scored vs Türkiye on debut; blistering pace is Australia's main counter threat against a high Paraguay line chasing the win. Real value on the break.
Box-arriving midfielder; late runs and second balls in a Paraguay side that must pour men forward.

assists.note: Chief creator and set-piece taker for Paraguay post-Almirón; most of Paraguay's chance creation routes through him.
Inverting attacking mid who links Australia's counters; can release Irankunda in transition.
Left-sided midfield progression; one of Paraguay's wide supply lines into the box.

penalty.backup: Gustavo Gómez (PAR); Jackson Irvine or Aiden O'Neill (AUS)
penalty.note: Almirón (regular taker) is suspended, so penalty and free-kick duty falls to Enciso, with captain Gustavo Gómez an option on dead balls/corners. A chasing Paraguay forcing play against a deep block raises spot-kick odds, but Australia defend the box with discipline and concede few clear penalties; Australia's taker would be Irvine or O'Neill.

34-yo Independiente target man given the No.9 role over Sanabria (bench); hold-up play over pace, and starved of Almirón's delivery.
Becomes the team's creative hub and primary set-piece/penalty taker with Almirón suspended — Paraguay's win bid largely rides on him unlocking a packed defence.
Retains the gloves over Mathew Ryan; already kept a clean sheet vs Türkiye, so this is continuity rather than a gamble — key to an Australia-clean-sheet/draw outcome.
Scored on World Cup debut vs Türkiye; his pace in transition is Australia's chief weapon against a Paraguay side forced to commit numbers forward.
Recently capped wildcard, scored vs Türkiye; pairs with Metcalfe as inverting support, linking Australia's low-block-to-counter game; Mabil and Leckie start on the bench.

win.reason: Germany field a near-first-choice XI (Neuer, Kimmich, Tah, Rudiger, Sane, Musiala, Wirtz, Havertz) after two dominant wins and 9 goals, so on quality they should edge a possession-heavy game. But Ecuador are the toughest defensive side in the field, having conceded just five goals in 18 CONMEBOL qualifiers and only two competitively under Beccacece, with Pacho, Hincapie and Ordonez forming a compact, low block. Ecuador must win to have any chance, but their attack has been blunt (0 goals in two games), so this projects as a grind Germany wins narrowly rather than comfortably. Expect roughly a 53-55% German win probability, leaving genuine room for a draw or a smash-and-grab Ecuador result.

scorers.note: Leads the line as the false-nine/striker and is Germany's primary penalty taker, so he carries both open-play and spot-kick scoring routes against a packed Ecuador box.
Has rediscovered form late in the season and already contributed in the group stage; his between-the-lines movement is Germany's best tool to unlock a deep block.
Back to starting after his long leg-injury recovery; dribbling in tight spaces is exactly what's needed versus a compact defence, though sharpness is a question after bench minutes.
Provides width and pace on the right with Gnabry out; his cut-inside shot is a live threat if Ecuador's full-backs tuck narrow.

assists.note: Germany's chief creator from the left/central pockets; most likely to thread the final ball against a low block.
Set-piece deliverer and deep playmaker from right-back; corners and switches are a key route to break down Ecuador.
Overlapping left-back whose crossing volume is a primary supply line if Germany pin Ecuador deep.

penalty.note: Havertz took both penalties at Euro 2024 and converted vs Ghana in March, making him Germany's first choice with Kimmich next in line; Ecuador's veteran Valencia is the established taker. A spot-kick is plausible given Germany's heavy possession into a crowded box but far from likely, hence low-medium.

playerNotes.note: In-form with 3 goals off the bench (Finland, Curacao, Ivory Coast) but starts on the bench again; he is Germany's most dangerous late-game changer if they need to chase a winner.
Starting after recovering from last July's broken leg/dislocated ankle; he has mostly played from the bench recently, so match sharpness over 90 minutes is uncertain and he may be subbed.
Came out of international retirement for the tournament; behind a side already qualified, he could face long spells of inactivity against Ecuador's low-volume attack.
Anchors a backline that conceded only five goals in 18 qualifiers; alongside Hincapie and Ordonez he forms the disciplined block that makes an Ecuador clean sheet and a draw genuinely live.
At 36 with 49 international goals, he is Ecuador's main outlet and penalty taker, but as an aging veteran he is explicitly not a banker scorer; his threat is on the break, not sustained pressure.

Ecuador's most creative wide forward and their best chance to produce the goal they need; Ecuador must win after a goalless draw with Curacao, so expect him to push higher than a pure containment game.

win.reason: Confirmed XIs change the read: Ivory Coast picked an aggressive 4-2-4 (Bonny + Pepe + Yan Diomande + Amad ahead of a Sangare-Kessie pivot) — they are hunting the win, not managing the draw they only need. Crucially Curacao MUST win to chase a best-third spot, so they cannot park a pure 0-0 bunker for 90 like they did vs Ecuador; once they commit bodies forward IC's pace punishes on the counter. That two-way need-to-win dynamic separates this from a flat possession-vs-block under. Real risk: Eloy Room made ~15-16 saves (WC record-tie) in their 0-0 vs Ecuador — if he repeats and IC's final ball is wasteful, a grinding 1-0 or even a draw is live (~25-30% combined). Away win likely (~68%) but not nailed-on.

scorers.note: Confirmed central striker on his competitive IC debut; most central-box touches vs a packed block, but a debutant lone-line role — value scorer, not a banker
Drifts inside off the right onto his left; IC's likeliest route to unlock a low block, scored the 90th-min winner vs Ecuador at this venue, also on free-kicks
RB Leipzig breakout who scored the winner vs Ecuador; direct dribbler stretching the block wide-left
Recalled veteran with cut-in goal threat but inconsistent and likely subbed ~60-65' — explicitly a NON-banker; corner taker

assists.note: Beat-a-man creator and free-kick deliverer; IC's chief chance generator vs a deep block
Deep-lying distributor and set-piece deliverer feeding Bonny and the runners
Overlapping right-back (in for the injured Singo) supplying width and cutbacks down IC's right

penalty.note: A deep, scrambling 5-4-1 defending its box for 90 minutes — especially late as Curacao tires and IC piles bodies in — carries elevated spot-kick risk (~30-35%). Kessie is IC's first taker, Sangare backup. A Curacao penalty is low-probability given little box time; it would fall to Leandro Bacuna, then Juninho.

Recalled veteran winger cutting in from the left and a corner taker; explicit NON-banker given inconsistency, likely subbed around the hour
Half of the double pivot that lets IC commit four attackers; designated penalty + free-kick taker and set-piece deliverer, must also screen the counter when Curacao pushes
Lone striker carrying a knock, isolated against Kossounou and Ousmane Diomande; must convert a rare chance for Curacao to have any path
Joint record cap-holder and Curacao's penalty + set-piece taker; their most plausible goal source is a Bacuna dead-ball against the IC block

win.pick: Draw (Japan advance) — lean Japan or Draw double chance
win.reason: Japan need only a draw to reach the Round of 32 while Sweden must win, a real draw-pull dynamic with the confirmed XI: Tomiyasu-Itakura-Ito back three plus Sano and Ao Tanaka screening gives Japan a disciplined block built to absorb and counter through Junya Ito, Doan and Kamada. Sweden's confirmed back three (Lagerbielke-Hien-Lindelof) was carved open 5-1 by the Netherlands and now has to chase the game, exposing space behind for Ueda and the wide runners. Sweden's Gyokeres-Isak duo carries genuine match-winning firepower, so a Sweden win is live, but the most probable single outcome is a tight draw or a narrow Japan win on the break.

scorers.note: Japan's confirmed lone striker and designated penalty taker; thrives running into space behind a high line, exactly what a chasing Sweden back three will offer.
Sweden's primary penalty taker and in elite club form (PL top scorer with Arsenal), but kept non-banker against a compact, organised Japan block that limits clean looks.
Second half of Sweden's elite front pairing and a backup penalty option; drops to link play and can punish transitions if Japan over-commit.
Advanced central role behind Ueda, on free-kick duty; arrives late into the box and is a transition outlet if Japan break on a chasing Sweden.

assists.note: Confirmed starter and a designated corner taker; his deliveries and wide running are Japan's main supply line into Ueda against an exposed back three.
Starts wide-right and shares corner duties; direct carrier who can release runners on the counter Japan want to play.
Sweden's right-sided runner and a set-piece/corner taker; his pace stretches Japan's left and feeds Gyokeres and Isak as Sweden push forward.

penalty.note: A must-win Sweden pushing numbers forward against a deep Japan block raises box-congestion and penalty potential at both ends; Ueda strikes for Japan, Gyokeres for Sweden with Isak as the secondary spot-kick option.

Starts as central defender in the back three; aerial and organisational anchor tasked with containing the Gyokeres-Isak duo.
Confirmed No.1; a strong shot-stopping display is central to Japan keeping the clean sheet/draw they need.
Confirmed to start in goal as Sweden's No.1 after the 5-1 loss to Netherlands; under pressure to steady a leaky back three.
Primary penalty taker and Sweden's talisman (19 goals in 32 caps, PL top scorer with Arsenal); scored in the 5-1 Tunisia win, the key man Japan must nullify.
Confirmed central midfielder (Union Saint-Gilloise); part of a reshaped midfield that must both create for the front two and protect an exposed defence as Sweden chase the win.

win.reason: Koeman makes just one change (Aké in for van de Ven) from the side that thrashed Sweden 5-1, keeping a near-full-strength front line of Gakpo, Brobbey and Malen against an already-eliminated Tunisia that has conceded nine in two games. Tunisia (now under Renard after Lamouchi's sacking) will likely sit in a deep block to restore pride, so a Dutch win is the clear lean — but a dead-rubber Netherlands chasing only seeding can ease off, and a disciplined low-block carries genuine draw/low-event risk, hence fairOdds well above 'banker' territory.

scorers.note: Scored a clinical brace inside 17 mins vs Sweden (5th and 17th); confirmed starting striker and the focal point against a porous Tunisia back line, but a deep block plus possible early substitution in a dead rubber adds risk.
Brace and Man of the Match vs Sweden, and the on-pitch penalty taker with Depay/Weghorst on the bench; threat from the left cutting in, but downgrade if Tunisia pack the box.
Arrives in scintillating form — 14 goals in 18 Serie A games for Roma — and starts on the right; pace and directness exploit a shaky Tunisia defence.
If Netherlands win a spot-kick, Gakpo takes it with Depay off; penalty likelihood is only low-medium against a deep block, hence the long price.

assists.note: Provided two assists vs Sweden and is the Dutch crossing/byline machine from right-back; deliveries into the box are the most likely source against a deep block.
Created Brobbey's opener vs Sweden with a left-sided cross; doubles as scorer and creator from the left.
Deep-lying creator who will see heavy possession against a passive Tunisia block; through-balls to Brobbey/Malen the likely route.

penalty.backup: Ali Abdi / Ismaël Gharbi (Tunisia); Donyell Malen (Netherlands)
penalty.note: Depay and Weghorst (usual Dutch first-choice spot-kicks) start on the bench, so Gakpo is the on-pitch taker (two pens vs Uzbekistan in warm-up). A deep, retreating Tunisia block keeps the Dutch penalty chance only low-medium; for Tunisia, Mejbri is listed first among their takers.

lineups.home: Aymen Dahmen, Ellyes Skhiri, Mohamed Amine Ben Hamida, Montassar Talbi, Ali Abdi, Yan Valery, Rani Khedira, Ismaël Gharbi, Hannibal Mejbri, Anis Slimane, Hazem Mastouri
lineups.away: Bart Verbruggen, Virgil van Dijk, Jan Paul van Hecke, Nathan Aké, Denzel Dumfries, Frenkie de Jong, Tijjani Reijnders, Ryan Gravenberch, Brian Brobbey, Cody Gakpo, Donyell Malen

playerNotes.note: Brace + MOTM vs Sweden (5 career WC goals); starts left of a front three and is the designated penalty taker with Depay benched — top scorer and assist threat in one.
Confirmed central striker; brace inside 17 mins vs Sweden, only the second pair of Dutch teammates to net multiples in a single WC match alongside Gakpo. Sunderland's PL forward.
Starts on the right after a stunning Roma half-season (14 goals in 18 Serie A games); pace and directness are a major mismatch versus Tunisia's full-backs.
Two assists vs Sweden; the Inter right-back is the primary width and crossing outlet, the most likely assist source against a packed box.
Tunisia's No.10 talisman and first-listed penalty taker; the creative outlet who must spring counters off Mastouri — their only realistic source of an upset moment.
Lone striker (Dynamo Makhachkala), 4 goals in 12 caps including vs Brazil; tasked with holding play in a deep block — the system's structural weak point if he can't retain possession.

win.reason: Egypt are the clear possession side and carry the individual edge through Salah, but Iran's 5-4-1 deep block under Ghalenoei is built to frustrate. With Marmoush benched and Trezeguet leading the line, Egypt lose some penetration. Win prob only ~50-52% — draw + Iran ~45%. This is a genuine break-down-the-block game, not a comfortable home win.

scorers.note: Egypt's sole penalty taker and primary free-kick taker; main goal route but faces a packed deep block and double-marking — non-banker.
Leads the line in the confirmed XI with Marmoush benched; scored vs New Zealand. Service-dependent against five at the back.
Scored and assisted last time out; corner taker and second-phase shooter from the right.
Iran's lone striker, penalty and free-kick taker, but isolated up top and 33 — counter/set-piece dependent. Explicitly NON-banker given age and isolation.

assists.note: Creator-in-chief; corners and open-play deliveries are Egypt's likeliest assist source against a low block.
Corner specialist and wide creator; assisted in the previous match.
Advanced midfielder threading the final pass and arriving in the box; secondary free-kick option.

penalty.note: Salah is Egypt's sole confirmed penalty taker and he is on the pitch (Marmoush, the other listed PK option, is benched). Iran's deep block concedes box contact, so a spot-kick is a live route. For Iran, Taremi takes any penalty they earn.

lineups.away: Alireza Beiranvand, Shoja Khalilzadeh, Ali Nemati, Hossein Kanani, Milad Mohammadi, Ramin Rezaeian, Saeid Ezatolahi, Mohammad Ghorbani, Mohammad Mohebbi, Saman Ghoddos, Mehdi Taremi

playerNotes.note: Sole penalty and primary free-kick taker; the focal point of every Egypt attack. Will be the most double-marked man on the pitch against Iran's five-at-the-back.
Starts as the lone striker with Marmoush dropped to the bench; impactful sub vs New Zealand now promoted. Effectiveness hinges on width and service breaking the block.
Scored and assisted in the last outing; takes corners and provides the right-sided creative threat and second-phase shooting.
Steps into the central pivot; key to recycling possession and screening against Iran's counters through Taremi.
Iran's isolated lone striker and penalty/free-kick taker; at 33 he is the entire attack but starved of support in the 5-4-1 — a counter and set-piece threat, not a volume scorer.
Kept a clean sheet with 7 saves vs Belgium; the deep block plus an in-form keeper makes Iran a real clean-sheet and under-2.5 risk, downgrading Egypt's win-to-nil and correct-score confidence.

Starting left forward; sharp movement and a reliable left-foot finish, Belgium's most clinical wide option.
Captain and chief creator; also the penalty taker with Lukaku off the pitch, plus a long-range and set-piece threat.
Recalled and starting right wing; explosive 1v1 dribbler who can carve a deep NZ block, more assist than scorer but can finish cut-ins.

assists.note: Primary creator and dead-ball taker (free kicks + shares corners with Tielemans); most likely source of the assist.
Beats his man and pulls the ball back; high chance-creation from the right flank against a deep block.
Attacking left-back who overlaps Trossard; delivery from the left a secondary supply line.

penalty.note: Lukaku (usual No.1 penalty taker) is on the bench, so De Bruyne steps up as on-pitch taker; Tielemans is the backup. A spot-kick is plausible if NZ defend deep and concede a box foul, but not guaranteed.

Captain and lone striker; NZ's entire attack runs through holding the ball up to him on the counter — NZ's only real route to a goal or a point.

win.reason: Cape Verde need only a draw to advance and will sit in a compact 4-1-4-1 deep block with Deroy Duarte screening and Willy Semedo/Kevin Pina tucking in, exactly the shape that produced a 0-0 vs Spain and held leads vs Uruguay. Saudi Arabia must win on one point and -4 goal difference, so they will over-commit Salem Al-Dawsari and the full-backs forward, which both stretches them on the counter and plays into Cape Verde's hands. Saudi are the on-paper favourite with Al-Buraikan and Mandash carrying real end product, but breaking a side that is content to draw is the hardest job in tournament football. A possession-favourite against a motivated deep block is a textbook draw trap, and Cape Verde's two prior results show they defend leads and stalemates well. Roughly a 38% Saudi win, 33% draw, 29% Cape Verde split feels fair, so there is no value in laying a short price on Saudi. The draw and draw-no-bet both carry genuine weight here.

scorers.note: Saudi's top WCQ scorer (5) and sharpest finisher with a reliable left foot; lone-ish striker who must convert if Saudi are to win
Captain and primary penalty taker, dropping behind Al-Brikan; chief creator-scorer Saudi will funnel everything through
Cuts in on his left foot from the right; six goals and five assists in his end-of-season Al-Hilal spell, Saudi's in-form attacker
Scored the longest, fastest free-kick of the round vs Uruguay; direct set-piece and counter threat from a deep midfield role

assists.note: Saudi's main creator and set-piece supplier; corners/free kicks plus open-play service to Al-Brikan
Five assists in his recent Al-Hilal run; wide delivery and cut-backs as Saudi chase the game
Captain, record scorer and a primary corner/free-kick taker; main outlet on Cape Verde counters

penalty.likelihood: moderate — Saudi must chase and will commit numbers, raising both box-entry and conceding odds on transitions
penalty.note: For Cape Verde, Ryan Mendes is the on-pitch penalty taker; Jamiro Monteiro is a secondary option.

lineups.away: Mohammed Al-Owais, Hassan Al-Tambakti, Abdulelah Al-Amri, Nawaf Boushal, Saud Abdulhamid, Nasser Al-Dawsari, Abdullah Al-Khaibari, Salem Al-Dawsari, Sultan Mandash, Mohamed Kanno, Feras Al-Brikan

playerNotes.note: Lone striker; scored 4 in WC qualifying and was the decisive scorer vs Cameroon, but went 0-for-28 on loan at Casa Pia this season, so his finishing is volatile against a deep-lying counter role
Starts wide in place of Garry Rodrigues; Omonia Nicosia winger with only 1 goal in 8 club games this term, more of a defensive-runner outlet than a goal threat in this block
Krasnodar midfielder who scored the round's fastest and longest goal, a 30m free kick vs Uruguay; carries the direct set-piece threat from a left-of-centre role
Ludogorets man operating as the single holding pivot; key ball-progressor and the screen that lets Cape Verde sit deep and absorb Saudi pressure
Al-Ahli striker and top WCQ scorer (5); sharp in tight spaces with a dependable left foot, Saudi's most likely route to the must-win goal
34-year-old captain, joint-top Saudi WC scorer and the primary penalty/free-kick taker; drops in behind Al-Brikan and is the focal creator Saudi must over-rely on

win.reason: Spain top the group, haven't conceded in two games, and with Rodri-Pedri-Merino controlling tempo and Oyarzabal (2g+1a vs Saudi) leading the line they are clear favourites at Opta's 62.2%. But Bielsa's Uruguay have drawn both games via a disciplined deep block, and with Baena (creator, not pure goal threat) starting wide over Nico Williams and Yamal only just back from hamstring, Spain's edge is real but not overwhelming. A possession-favourite vs an organised counter side carries genuine draw risk (Opta 22.1%); 55-62% is not certainty.

scorers.note: Spain's central #9 and first-choice penalty taker; 2g+1a vs Saudi, the safest goalscorer signal but facing a packed Uruguay block.
Right-wing threat and Spain's biggest dribbler, but only just back from hamstring and unlikely to play 90; backup set-piece option.
Late box-arriving runs and aerial threat from midfield; 6 qualifying goals incl. headers makes him a live set-piece scorer vs a side that can be got at in the air.
Bielsa's clinical #9 over Vinas; pace-and-chaos runner who is Uruguay's main outlet on the counter, but isolated against Cubarsi-Laporte.

assists.note: Starting wide-left over Nico Williams as Spain's elite left-footed creator (La Liga assist leader history); chief chance-supplier in open play.
Spain's metronome who threads the final-third passes; high involvement against a deep block.
Crosses and cutbacks from the right were Spain's main creation route vs Saudi; assist threat even if minutes are managed.

penalty.likelihood: medium - Spain dominate the ball and draw fouls vs a deep block, raising spot-kick odds, though Uruguay defend the box well
penalty.note: Spain: Oyarzabal first choice, Rodri/Yamal as backups. Uruguay: Valverde primary (also FKs and corners), Nunez secondary.

lineups.away: Unai Simón, Aymeric Laporte, Pau Cubarsí, Marc Cucurella, Marcos Llorente, Mikel Merino, Rodri, Pedri , Mikel Oyarzabal, Álex Baena, Lamine Yamal

playerNotes.note: Big selection call: starts wide-left over Nico Williams. He is a creator/assist-maker (former La Liga assist leader), not a high-volume goal threat, so Spain's left side is more chance-creation than direct verticality.
Confirmed central #9 and first-choice penalty taker; came alive vs Saudi with 2 goals + 1 assist after a quiet debut. Spain's safest goalscorer but must break a stubborn block.
Just back from an April hamstring injury, not at 100% and unlikely to finish 90; still Spain's most dangerous 1v1 wide threat and a growing set-piece/penalty backup.
Underrated late-runner and aerial scorer from midfield (6 goals in qualifying, headers vs Bulgaria/Turkiye); a key set-piece weapon against Uruguay.
Bielsa picks him as the clinical #9 over Vinas; the 'Agent of Chaos' is Uruguay's primary outlet on the counter but his club form is inconsistent and he'll be isolated against Cubarsi-Laporte.
Captain and set-piece hub: takes penalties, direct free-kicks and corners, plays advanced behind the striker, and carries the long-range shooting threat. Uruguay's most versatile prop target.

win.reason: France keep Mbappe, Dembele, Olise and Doue ahead of Tchouameni-Rabiot, but Saliba is out (Lacroix in) and Norway's heavily-rotated B-XI may sit deep with Berg-Aursnes-Aasgaard screening. A low-stakes dead rubber for both: real draw/under risk, France only ~52-55%.

scorers.note: France captain and penalty taker; led the line vs Senegal/Iraq and remains the primary finisher against a makeshift Lacroix-Upamecano pairing with Saliba out.
Red-hot: matched Mbappe's record with a recent hat-trick and scored in the group stage; cuts in from the right and is France's secondary penalty/scoring threat.
Starts wide-left in the 4-2-3-1; direct dribbler who can punish Bjorkan one-v-one and arrive at the back post.
Lone striker with Haaland rested; Norway's nominal No.9 and a penalty candidate in this XI, but service is thin without Odegaard feeding him.

assists.note: France's chief creator from the right; assisted Mbappe vs Senegal with a precision pass and takes attacking set-pieces.
Beats his man and cuts back; likely supplier for Mbappe or Doue arriving centrally.
Norway's best transition outlet and an in-XI free-kick/corner taker now that Odegaard is rested; likeliest source of a Norwegian goal contribution.

penalty.likelihood: medium - France's pressure on a deep, rotated Norway back line invites a box infringement, though a dead-rubber tempo lowers intensity
penalty.note: With Haaland and Odegaard both rested, Norway's spot-kick would fall to a striker/set-piece taker in this XI - Jorgen Strand Larsen or Kristian Thorstvedt, with Oscar Bobb on free-kicks/corners; NOT Haaland.

lineups.home: Egil Selvik, Leo Østigard, Henrik Falchener, Fredrik André Bjørkan, Fredrik Aursnes, Patrick Berg, Thelo Aasgaard, Kristian Thorstvedt, Jørgen Strand Larsen, Andreas Schjelderup, Oscar Bobb
lineups.away: Mike Maignan, Maxence Lacroix, Dayot Upamecano, Theo Hernández, Jules Koundé, Michael Olise, Manu Koné, Aurélien Tchouaméni, Kylian Mbappé, Désiré Doué, Ousmane Dembélé

playerNotes.note: RESTED on the bench - Solbakken protects his fitness for the knockouts since topping Group I offers little Round-of-32 advantage. Could appear late, but is NOT a starter and not on set-pieces/penalties tonight.
RESTED on the bench - captain and creative engine sat down; Norway drop the 4-3-3 for a 4-2-3-1, gutting their chance-creation and set-piece delivery in this XI.
Crystal Palace striker leads the line in Haaland's absence; a penalty candidate but isolated without Odegaard's supply behind him.
Fulham winger; Norway's best transition threat and an in-XI free-kick/corner taker - the likeliest Norwegian to create or score on the break.
Captain, penalty taker and focal point; 64 career pens scored. Faces a patched-up Lacroix-Upamecano centre-back pairing with Saliba out.
Steps in for the injured/absent Saliba alongside Upamecano - the key defensive downgrade and the reason France's win-to-nil line is shaded down.

facts: Norway: 6 pts, +1 GD across 3 group games (8 for / 7 against).
France: 9 pts, +8 GD across 3 group games (10 for / 2 against).
Norway's Erling Haaland leads them with 7 goals in 4 games.
France's Kylian Mbappé leads them with 7 goals in 5 games.
assumptions: Model projects France to carry more of the ball and the better chances (higher attack rating); Norway to sit compact and play on the break.
Goal expectancy 1.4–3.5 (Norway–France) assumes group-stage scoring rates hold — a big regression risk on a 2–3 game sample.

win.reason: Senegal must win for third-place hopes and will dominate possession with Sarr, Mane and 18yo PSG scorer I.Mbaye against a deep Iraq block. But this is no certainty: Iraq sit deep and counter via Al-Hamadi and Bayesh, and Senegal's back line is makeshift - Koulibaly benched, debutant Diaw in goal for the injured Mendy, Seck partnering Niakhate. A possession-favourite vs organised low block is the classic frustration/draw trap; Iraq scored vs both Norway and France. Mane is 33-34 and not a guaranteed scorer. Senegal are roughly 55-60% to win, with a real share for the draw and the under-2.5 outcome.

scorers.note: In-form Crystal Palace winger scored twice vs Norway; Senegal's sharpest current finisher, attacks Iraq's right-back from the left/right channel
18yo PSG winger who scored vs France; pace and flair, now a starter and live threat cutting in at Iraq's full-backs
Drifts in off the left and is primary penalty taker, but 33-34 and not a banker - aging marquee forwards blank; medium threat
Ipswich (PL) striker leading the line with Hussein out; Iraq's lone outlet on the counter against a makeshift Senegal back four

assists.note: Creator-in-chief from the left, set up Sarr vs France; likely on corners/cutbacks
Orchestrates through the middle and is a set-piece delivery option with Diouf benched
Overlapping right-back/wide outlet who can deliver into the box against a deep block

penalty.likelihood: medium - Senegal will draw fouls attacking a packed Iraq box, but a deep block also concedes fewer clear pen situations
penalty.note: With Koulibaly and Jackson benched, Mané is the confirmed primary penalty taker; Sarr is the established backup. Iraq's taker is Al-Ammari.

lineups.home: Mory Diaw, Moussa Niakhaté, Abdoulaye Seck, Ismail Jakobs, Krépin Diatta, Idrissa Gueye, Lamine Camara, Habib Diarra, Ismaïla Sarr, Sadio Mané, Ibrahim Mbaye
lineups.away: Ahmed Basil, Akam Hashim, Rebin Sulaka, Merchas Doski, Frans Putros, Ali Jasim, Amir Al-Ammari, Zidane Iqbal, Ali Al-Hamadi, Ibrahim Bayesh, Ahmed Qasim

playerNotes.note: Captain BENCHED after costly errors vs Norway; Seck partners Niakhaté instead. Leaves a makeshift, less commanding centre-back pairing that Iraq can target on the counter - a key reason Senegal's win-to-nil is overrated.

BENCHED; I.Mbaye starts as the wide/central forward instead of a recognised No.9. Senegal go without an orthodox target striker, so goals likely come from wide runners (Sarr, Mbaye, Mané) not a central poacher.
Starts in goal for the injured Édouard Mendy - a downgrade in pedigree behind a reshuffled defence; adds to the case against backing a clean sheet.
Best current form in the XI - scored twice vs Norway. Primary goal threat and backup penalty taker; pace stretches Iraq's deep line.
Ipswich Town striker, leads the line with Aymen Hussein injured. Iraq's main counter outlet; clever movement, but isolated against a possession-dominant Senegal - Iraq's chances will be rare.

Runs ALL Iraq set pieces (corners + free kicks) AND is the penalty taker. With Hussein's aerial threat gone, his deliveries now target Putros/Hashem/Al-Hamadi - Iraq's main remaining set-piece danger.

Iraq: 0 pts, -11 GD across 3 group games (1 for / 12 against).
Senegal's Ismaïla Sarr leads them with 4 goals in 4 games.
Iraq's Aymen Hussein leads them with 1 goal in 1 game.

win.reason: Both sides sit on 3 points and a draw likely sends each through (Austria 2nd on goal difference, Algeria as a best third-placed side), which removes urgency and pushes toward a cagey game; Austria's Seiwald-X.Schlager double pivot plus Alaba/Lienhart is built to absorb, while Algeria's flair through Mahrez, Aouar and Gouiri can be neutralised by a deep block. With Algeria fielding backup keeper Benbot and neither team strictly needing to chase, the most probable single outcome is a low-scoring stalemate rather than a clear winner.

scorers.note: Algeria's lead striker and the man who scored the late winner vs Jordan; sharp on second balls and from corners, the most likely Algerian goal threat with Amoura injured.
Captain and primary set-piece/corner taker; his inswinging deliveries created both Algeria goals vs Jordan and he carries the penalty/free-kick threat from the right.
Austria's most complete midfielder and the free-kick/corner taker; arrives late into the box and is the secondary penalty option behind Arnautovic.
Record scorer and lone striker, first-choice penalty taker, but he is 37 and a rotational/fading presence — a genuine threat from the spot or a set-piece, NOT a banker anytime scorer.

assists.note: Algeria's chief creator and dead-ball supplier; his corners/free kicks are the most direct assist route given Algeria's reliance on set pieces.
Central creator in the 4-3-3, threads the final ball between the lines and links Mahrez and Gouiri; Algeria's open-play creativity runs through him.
Shares corner duties and steps out from centre-back to deliver set pieces; Austria's aerial goal threat (Lienhart, Arnautovic) often originates from his deliveries.

penalty.note: Penalty likelihood medium in a tight knockout-stakes game. Mahrez is Algeria's captain and primary dead-ball man; for Austria the spot-kick would fall to Arnautovic, then Sabitzer.

lineups.home: Oussama Benbot, Rafik Belghali, Aïssa Mandi, Ramy Bensebaini, Jaouen Hadjam, Ibrahim Maza, Nabil Bentaleb, Houssem Aouar, Riyad Mahrez, Amine Gouiri, Farès Chaïbi
lineups.away: Alexander Schlager, Stefan Posch, Philipp Lienhart, David Alaba, Phillipp Mwene, Nicolas Seiwald, Xaver Schlager, Romano Schmid, Konrad Laimer, Marcel Sabitzer, Marko Arnautovic

playerNotes.note: Backup keeper given the start as Algeria rotate with one eye on the draw scenario; less proven than the first-choice, a slight downgrade behind an experienced Mandi-Bensebaini pairing.
In form — scored the 82nd-minute winner vs Jordan and leads the line with Amoura injured; thrives on cut-backs and corner scraps.
Captain, right wing, and set-piece engine; his inswinging corners directly created both goals vs Jordan and he is the main creative and dead-ball threat.
The fulcrum of Austria's midfield and free-kick/corner taker; technically the most complete player in the side and a late-arriving goal threat.
37-year-old record scorer (47 goals) and first-choice penalty taker, but a fading lone striker isolated against Mandi/Bensebaini — capable but rotational, NOT a banker.
The engine of Rangnick's press, covers huge ground and sets tempo; his energy will try to choke Aouar and Bentaleb in central midfield.

win.reason: Heavily-rotated Argentina without Messi (benched, rested, can come on ~60-70') still field Lautaro Martinez, Julian Alvarez, Otamendi and a Paredes-Palacios-Lo Celso core - clearly superior to an eliminated Jordan side. But this is a dead rubber (Argentina already won Group J) so intensity is down, and Jordan defend deep in a 3-4-3; that caps the margin and adds genuine draw/under risk. Win probability sits around 60-65%, not a lock.

scorers.note: Leads the anytime line with Messi benched; yet to score this WC and motivated. Shares penalty duty - on-pitch taker tonight.
Strike partner alongside Lautaro; busy presser, also goalless this tournament and hungry vs a tiring deep block.
Advanced playmaker behind the two strikers; arriving-late shots and set-piece edge of box.
Jordan's lone striker and scorer of their first-ever WC goal vs Austria; main outlet on the counter.

assists.note: Chief creator in this XI feeding Lautaro/Alvarez.
Left-side link, deliveries and through-balls into the box.
Overlapping LB whipping crosses against Jordan's right wing-back.

penalty.note: With Messi benched, Lautaro is the on-pitch penalty taker (shares duty with Messi normally; Messi missed vs Austria). If Messi comes on ~60-70' he likely reclaims spot-kick duty. Alvarez is the secondary option.

lineups.home: Yazeed Abu Laila, Abdallah Nasib, Yazan Al-Arab, Husam Ali Mohammad, Ehsan Haddad, Nizar Al-Rashdan, Noor Al-Rawabdeh, Mohannad Abu Taha, Ali Al-Azaizeh, Ali Olwan, Odeh Fakhoury
lineups.away: Emiliano Martinez, Giuliano Simeone, Nicolas Otamendi, Marcos Senesi, Nicolas Tagliafico, Nico Paz, Leandro Paredes, Exequiel Palacios, Giovani Lo Celso, Julian Alvarez, Lautaro Martinez

playerNotes.note: STARTS ON THE BENCH - rested by Scaloni, not injured, after Argentina already won Group J. Scored all 5 of Argentina's WC goals so far and is the all-time WC top scorer (18). Expected on ~60-70'; would likely reclaim penalty/free-kick duty if introduced. Live sub threat, not a starter pick.
Confirmed starter and lead striker; on-pitch penalty taker with Messi out. Goalless this tournament and motivated - the prime anytime-scorer pick, but kept banker:false given dead-rubber intensity.
Starts up top alongside Lautaro. Also yet to score at this WC; relentless presser who profits from a tiring deep block, secondary penalty option.
Starts as the advanced creator in midfield with Messi rested - the chief chance-maker tonight, top assist pick and a late-arriving goal threat.
Jordan's lone forward and scorer of their first-ever World Cup goal (vs Austria). Main counter-attack outlet against a rotated, lower-intensity Argentina back line.
Starting wide-forward and one of Jordan's primary set-piece/free-kick takers - their best route to a goal is a dead-ball delivery into Emiliano Martinez's box.

win.reason: Confirmed XIs point to a cagey Group-K finale: Colombia (Vargas; Arias, Sanchez, Lucumi, Machado; Puerta, Lerma, J.Arias; James, Diaz, Cordoba) are already through and only need a draw to top the group, so they can sit in a mid-block and counter through Diaz/James. Portugal fielded a control midfield (Ruben Neves + Vitinha + Bruno) and Ronaldo (41) up top, the same shape that produced just 0.64 xG despite 75% possession vs DR Congo. Possession-vs-conservative-opponent is a textbook draw/under spot; neither win-to-nil line is safe. Model win-prob near 55% would still be no certainty here.

scorers.note: Colombia's sharpest threat in transition off the left; if the game opens he is the most likely Colombian scorer, but a deep-block plan caps his volume.
Confirmed to start ahead of Suarez as the No.9; physical focal point and a header/rebound threat, but service may be limited if Colombia plays for the draw.
Portugal's de-facto creator-finisher from midfield arrivals and set-pieces; against a low block his late-box runs and direct free-kicks are Portugal's most repeatable goal route.
Starts and is the penalty taker, but at 41 he is explicitly a non-banker: open-play involvement vs a packed box is low-volume and he relies on a spot-kick or one tap-in.

assists.note: Colombia's chief creator and dead-ball deliverer; even in a conservative setup his set-pieces are the primary assist source for Cordoba/Sanchez/Lucumi.
Portugal's main chance-creator from open play and corners; most likely to tee up Ronaldo or a back-post arrival.
Direct wide threat tasked with beating Machado and crossing for Ronaldo; his cut-backs are Portugal's likeliest wide assist.

penalty.note: Ronaldo is Portugal's confirmed first-choice penalty taker with Bruno Fernandes the backup. For Colombia the spot-kick falls to James Rodriguez (backup Luis Diaz) now that Daniel Munoz is NOT in the XI - the prior Munoz pick is void. Likelihood only medium: a draw-managing, deep-block game produces fewer box duels and VAR awards.

lineups.home: Camilo Vargas, Santiago Arias, Deiver Machado, Davinson Sánchez, Jhon Lucumí, Gustavo Puerta, Jhon Arias, Jefferson Lerma, Luis Díaz, James Rodríguez, Jhon Córdoba
lineups.away: Diogo Costa, Nuno Mendes, Rúben Dias, Renato Veiga, João Cancelo, Vitinha , Rúben Neves, Pedro Neto, Bruno Fernandes, João Félix, Cristiano Ronaldo

playerNotes.note: Starts at RB over Daniel Munoz (confirmed) - a more conservative pick signalling Colombia is set up to protect a draw and limit Nuno Mendes/Felix down their right.
Confirmed at LB ahead of Mojica; tested by Pedro Neto's directness, so his 1v1 defending is a key matchup and he offers less overlap than an attacking full-back.
Gets the nod up front over Luis Suarez - a target-man to hold play and attack James' set-pieces, reinforcing a pragmatic, low-chance game plan.
It is RUBEN Neves (Al Hilal), not Joao Neves, anchoring the pivot - a deeper control-and-recycle profile that fits Portugal dominating possession but risks the slow, low-xG buildup seen vs DR Congo.
Confirmed starting at 41 and is the penalty taker; treat as a non-banker scorer - low open-play volume against a packed box, dependent on a spot-kick or single tap-in.
Colombia's creative hub and the real penalty taker now Munoz is benched; his dead-ball delivery is Colombia's single most dangerous repeatable weapon in an otherwise cautious setup.

sources: Method: cross-checked live confirmed-lineup sources to resolve predicted-vs-confirmed conflicts. Confirmed Santiago Arias (RB) and Deiver Machado (LB) over Munoz/Mojica, Jhon Cordoba over Suarez up front, and RUBEN Neves (not Joao Neves) in Portugal's pivot with Ronaldo starting. Penalty taker reassigned from Munoz (benched) to James Rodriguez (Colombia) and Ronaldo (Portugal).

win.reason: DR Congo (Mbemba, Wan-Bissaka, Tuanzebe, Masuaku) showed real defensive resilience drawing Portugal 1-1 with Wissa scoring, and their physical Bakambu+Wissa front two test Uzbekistan's three-man back line; but Uzbekistan are organised and can sit deep and counter through Fayzullaev/Shomurodov, so the favourite is far from certain.

scorers.note: Scored DR Congo's first-ever World Cup goal vs Portugal; sharper movement than Bakambu and the stronger DRC scorer pick against a 3-man back line, though limited minutes at Newcastle after a knee injury keep odds realistic.
Scored Uzbekistan's first-ever World Cup goal equalising vs Colombia; the creative 10 and main counter-attacking threat, also on free kicks and corners.
Captain, all-time top scorer (44 goals) and designated penalty taker, but still chasing his first World Cup goal as the lone striker isolated against Mbemba/Tuanzebe.
Team-leading 21 international goals and most active DRC attacker by touches, plus a designated penalty taker; at 34 a veteran marquee striker, so not a banker.

assists.note: Uzbekistan's chief creator on the counter plus all set pieces (corners/free kicks), the most likely supply line to Shomurodov.
Attacking left-back providing width and crosses against Uzbekistan's narrow back three; debutant Mbuku ahead of him stretches that flank.
Making his first DR Congo start on the wing; pace and delivery into Bakambu/Wissa make him a live crossing/assist source.

penalty.note: Uzbekistan pens taken by captain Shomurodov; DR Congo's designated takers are Bakambu and Wissa (Mbemba converted the decisive qualifying shootout pen vs Nigeria). Medium likelihood with two cautious-aggressive sides and box congestion against a back three.

lineups.home: Lionel Mpasi, Chancel Mbemba, Arthur Masuaku, Aaron Wan-Bissaka, Axel Tuanzebe, Noah Sadiki, Brian Cipenga, Nathanaël Mbuku, Samuel Moutoussamy, Cédric Bakambu, Yoane Wissa
lineups.away: Abduvohid Nematov, Abdukodir Khusanov, Jakhongir Urozov, Rustam Ashurmatov, Otabek Shukurov, Khojiakbar Alijonov, Sherzod Nasrullaev, Dostonbek Khamdamov, Abbosbek Fayzullaev, Akmal Mozgovoy, Eldor Shomurodov

playerNotes.note: Captain and record cap-holder, anchors the back four that held Portugal to one goal; the organiser tasked with smothering Shomurodov.
Premier League right-back providing defensive width and overlaps; key to containing Uzbekistan's left-side counters while supporting attacks.
Energetic central/wide midfielder in the 4-4-2, ball-winning and transition engine linking defence to the Bakambu-Wissa front two.
Manchester City defender anchoring the three-man back line; top-level pace and composure are Uzbekistan's steadying presence against the physical DRC strikers, played full 90 in both losses.
Captain, talisman and lone striker; primary penalty taker still hunting a first World Cup goal, will feed off scraps on the counter.
Centre-back in the back three who must marshal the line against Bakambu/Wissa; Uzbekistan conceded heavily in their opener so his discipline is pivotal.

sources: ESPN FIFA World Cup summary feed (confirmed team sheet)

win.reason: England's confirmed 4-2-3-1 with Saka, Rashford, Rogers and Kane carries clear quality and wide overloads vs Panama's deep 5-4-1 block. But Panama held Croatia to 0-1 and will sit deep to frustrate; chance-creation against a low block is not guaranteed, so this is a genuine ~60-64% win, not a certainty — the draw and a 1-0 grind both have real weight.

scorers.note: Captain and penalty taker; scored twice vs Croatia (one pen). Primary scorer but drops deep vs a low block, so chances can be scrappy — banker:false for safety.
Starts on the right and now England's main corner taker with Rice benched; wide overload vs Panama's LWB Gutiérrez is England's cleanest edge.
Came off bench to score vs Croatia; now starts LW, attacking Panama's RWB Murillo. Cutting in onto his right is a real goal route.
Confirmed at the 10; 10 PL goals + Europa League Player of the Season form. Late runs from midfield and long-range shooting make him a live threat against a packed box.

assists.note: Set up Rashford vs Croatia; corners plus right-wing cutbacks make him England's top assist source.
Drops deep to link play; his lay-offs and flick-ons feed Rogers/Bellingham runners against a deep block.
In the deeper pivot tonight but still drives forward; scored vs Croatia and can slide through-balls for Kane/Saka.

penalty.note: Panama's deep 5-4-1 block invites England pressure in the box; Kane is the confirmed penalty taker (most pens in WC history). Medium not high — Panama defend honestly and England may not earn one.

lineups.home: Orlando Mosquera, José Córdoba, Andrés Andrade, Fidel Escobar, Jorge Gutiérrez, Amir Murillo, Édgar Yoel Bárcenas, Carlos Harvey, José Luis Rodríguez, Cristian Martínez, Tomás Rodríguez
lineups.away: Jordan Pickford, Marc Guéhi, Ezri Konsa, Elliot Anderson, Nico O'Reilly, Jarell Quansah, Jude Bellingham, Morgan Rogers, Marcus Rashford, Bukayo Saka, Harry Kane

playerNotes.note: Confirmed start at the 10 over Palmer; 10 PL goals + Europa League Player of the Season. Late runs and long shots make him England's variable goal threat to unlock a packed box.
Now primary corner taker with Rice on the bench; right-wing overload vs LWB Jorge Gutiérrez is England's cleanest creative edge. Assisted Rashford vs Croatia.
Promoted to start LW after his super-sub goal vs Croatia. Attacks RWB Amir Murillo, cutting inside onto his right foot.
Captain, penalty taker and most prolific WC penalty scorer. Drops deep to link, so against a deep block his service work may matter as much as his finishing.
Panama's lone striker and main outlet on the counter; isolated against Konsa/Guéhi, his hold-up and transition runs are Panama's slim route to a goal.
Wide-midfield counter outlet and dead-ball option in the confirmed XI; alongside José Luis Rodríguez he carries Panama's break threat and feeds Tomás Rodríguez.

win.reason: Confirmed XIs: Croatia (4-2-3-1) field Budimir up top with Modric (201st cap) and Kovacic anchoring, the unchanged Panama backline; Ghana (4-1-4-1) keep Asare after their clean sheet vs England, with Partey shielding and Williams/Semenyo as the counter outlet. Classic possession-favourite vs Queiroz deep-block: Ghana need only avoid defeat (4 pts) and have conceded zero this tournament, while Croatia must win from 3rd. Model gives Ghana ~47% but ~53% sits with draw+Croatia, so the result is a genuine toss-up. Ghana's last four games all went under 2 goals and they'll happily sit for a point, making the draw the live primary play rather than either win.

scorers.note: Confirmed to start ahead of Musa after scoring the Panama winner off the bench; penalty-box poacher and aerial threat who feeds off Perisic/Baturina service and is a designated backup pen taker.
Ghana's most dangerous attacker (62.5m Man City move, 10 PL goals first half of season); main pace-and-power counter outlet against a high Croatia line.
Starts wide-left in the 3-band; arrives at the back post on crosses and is a corner/set-piece threat plus an alternate penalty option.
Captain and lone striker, 7 goals/7 assists in qualifying and Ghana's confirmed penalty taker — most of his realistic output here is from the spot given Ghana's deep setup.

assists.note: Croatia's chief crosser from the left and corner taker; most likely supply line to Budimir.
Starting No.10 / creative fulcrum between midfield and Budimir; on corner duty alongside Modric and Perisic.
Pace on the break; most of Ghana's chance creation comes from Williams/Semenyo transitions to release Ayew.

penalty.backup: Mohammed Kudus (off bench) / Inaki Williams on-pitch for Ghana; Luka Modric for Croatia
penalty.note: Ghana's Ayew is a proven, ice-cold spot-kick taker (AFCON shootout vs Senegal, multiple qualifier conversions). For Croatia, Modric is the primary penalty taker with Budimir/Perisic as alternates. With Ghana likely sitting deep and Croatia overloading the box, a Croatia-favouring penalty shout is the more probable source — but overall likelihood is only medium in a tight low-block game.

lineups.home: Dominik Livakovic, Marin Pongracic, Josip Sutalo, Ivan Perisic, Josip Stanisic, Petar Sucic, Mateo Kovacic, Luka Modric, Ante Budimir, Martin Baturina, Nikola Vlasic
lineups.away: Benjamin Asare, Derrick Luckassen, Jonas Adjetey, Thomas Partey, Gideon Mensah, Marvin Senaya, Kwasi Sibo, Elisha Owusu, Kamaldeen Sulemana, Antoine Semenyo, Jordan Ayew

playerNotes.note: Promoted to starter over Petar Musa on the back of his match-winning sub goal vs Panama; pure penalty-box striker who will look to attack crosses against Ghana's compact centre-backs.
Makes his 201st cap, primary free-kick taker and a Croatia penalty option; at 40 he is the metronome but is NOT a banker scorer — treat any goal threat as a bonus, not a base case.
Sits as the deeper of the double pivot, carrying the ball through midfield to break Ghana's block; key to whether Croatia can sustain pressure.
Lone holding midfielder screening the back four — the hinge of Queiroz's low block; his positioning is central to Ghana's clean-sheet ambition.
Top counter-attacking weapon with pace and power; on transitions against Croatia's high line he is Ghana's likeliest scorer or chance creator.
Keeps the gloves after a clean sheet vs England; Ghana have conceded zero this tournament, reinforcing the under-2.5 / draw-no-bet case for the Black Stars.

win.reason: Canada are the on-paper favourites with Eustaquio controlling midfield and David in hat-trick form (6-0 vs Qatar), but South Africa's deep block and counter pace through Maseko/Mofokeng/Appollis make this a genuine draw/low-scoring risk; possession-favourite vs disciplined underdog is a classic out-xG trap, so Canada's edge is real but far from certain (~50%).

scorers.note: Canada's talisman and all-time top scorer (42 in 80); hat-trick vs Qatar and the penalty taker, so any spot-kick is his - but faced with a packed SA box and Williams behind it, no banker.
First start since the Bosnia opener; pace and runs in behind alongside David, lively outlet if SA's deep line steps up to counter.
SA's lone-ish target man; he gets the counter-attack chances (had a follow-up saved vs Korea) - the one realistic SA route to goal against Canada's press.
Scored the historic 63rd-min winner vs South Korea that sent SA through; quick wide threat on the break, a live scorer if SA spring a counter.

assists.note: Canada captain back in the XI; the deep-lying creator who feeds David and the runners, plus set-piece delivery.
Overlapping right-back delivering crosses into David/Oluwaseyi; Canada's width comes from the full-backs against a narrow SA block.
SA's most creative spark; carries the ball on the counter and links with Makgopa/Maseko - the assist source if SA break.

penalty.backup: Teboho Mokoena (SA designated taker; Appollis backup)
penalty.note: David earns and converts Canada's penalties (most fouled in the box, 27 club pens). For SA, Mokoena is the designated taker with Appollis backup. Crucially, if level after ET it goes to pens - Ronwen Williams is an elite shootout keeper (4 saves in one AFCON 2023 shootout, tournament best GK), a genuine SA edge.

lineups.home: Ronwen Williams, Khuliso Mudau, Ime Okon, Mbekezeli Mbokazi, Aubrey Modiba, Sphephelo Sithole, Teboho Mokoena, Thapelo Maseko, Relebohile Mofokeng, Oswin Appollis, Evidence Makgopa
lineups.away: Maxime Crépeau, Alistair Johnston, Derek Cornelius, Moïse Bombito, Richie Laryea, Tajon Buchanan, Stephen Eustáquio, Nathan Saliba, Liam Millar, Jonathan David, Tani Oluwaseyi

playerNotes.note: Hat-trick vs Qatar (6-0) after a quiet Bosnia opener; penalty taker and the focal point - but his pre-tournament form dipped (2 goals in 9, both pens), so streaky rather than nailed-on.
Makes his first WC start, slotting in beside Cornelius for De Fougerolles; physical CB, but a fresh partnership SA's counter pace could test early.
Captain returns to the XI after starting the Switzerland loss on the bench; restores midfield control and tempo - key to Canada breaking the deep block.
Sundowns winger, 22, scored the 63rd-min winner vs South Korea to make history; SA's quickest counter outlet and a real wide goal threat.
The lone-ish target man up top; SA defend deep and feed him on the break - gets the chances but service is thin against Canada's press.
Captain and elite shootout keeper - saved 4 penalties in a single AFCON 2023 shootout and won best-GK; the single biggest SA edge if this reaches penalties after ET.

win.reason: Brazil are clear favourites with Vinícius Júnior in scorching form (scored in all 3 group games, 4-5 goals) and Cunha (3 goals) as a mobile false-nine, but this is NOT a formality. Japan set up as a disciplined deep block that springs lethal vertical counters, and Brazil losing Raphinha's set-piece/penalty delivery and starting raw teenager Rayan on the right blunts one attacking lane. Model win-prob sits ~55-57%, leaving a combined ~43-45% for a Japan upset or draw — so the price is closer to 1.80 than a short-priced banker.

scorers.note: Scored in all three group games (4 goals); Brazil's primary left-side threat and corner/free-kick option now that Raphinha is out. Top scorer pick but never a banker.
Three goals in the group stage including a brace vs Haiti; mobile central nine who drops to combine. Real anytime threat but anytime-scorer banker:false rule applies.
Japan's danger man — brace vs Tunisia, 16 international goals, Eredivisie top scorer. Against a high Brazil line he's the most likely Japan scorer on the counter; underpins the Japan-to-score angle.
Confirmed penalty taker with Raphinha out, plus a left-eight who arrives late in the box; carries the spot-kick equity that lifts his scorer price.

assists.note: Creates as much as he finishes from the left; likeliest Brazil assist source feeding Cunha centrally.
Now a primary set-piece/free-kick deliverer alongside the corner duty with Raphinha absent; line-breaking passes from deep.
Japan's quickest outlet on the counter; if Brazil's full-backs overcommit he is the most likely supplier for Ueda/Maeda.

penalty.note: Raphinha (Brazil's first-choice penalty taker) is OUT injured, so Paquetá is the reported on-pitch taker, with Vinícius the likely backup. Penalty probability is only medium — Japan defend with positional containment and avoid rash challenges in the box.

lineups.home: Brazil (4-3-3): Alisson; Danilo, Marquinhos (C), Gabriel Magalhães, Douglas Santos; Casemiro, Bruno Guimarães, Lucas Paquetá; Rayan, Matheus Cunha, Vinícius Júnior. (Raphinha OUT injured.)
lineups.away: Japan: Zion Suzuki; Takehiro Tomiyasu, Shogo Taniguchi, Hiroki Ito; Kaishu Sano, Daichi Kamada, Junya Ito, Keito Nakamura; Ritsu Doan (C), Daizen Maeda, Ayase Ueda. (Itakura & Ao Tanaka NOT starting; Kubo OUT.)

playerNotes.note: In the form of the tournament — scored in all three group matches (4 goals). Primary creator and finisher and now a key set-piece/corner option with Raphinha out. The match's single biggest edge for Brazil.

Operating as the mobile nine with 3 goals (brace vs Haiti). Drops between lines, which against Japan's compact mid-block could either unlock space or get crowded out — do not bank him as an anytime scorer.

Teenage Bournemouth/Vasco right winger deputising for the injured Raphinha; started vs Scotland. Fast and direct but raw on a knockout stage — Brazil's least proven attacking lane and a reason the Brazil attack is slightly downgraded from full strength.
Reported penalty taker now that Raphinha is absent; a left-sided eight who arrives late in the box. Carries the spot-kick and secondary-scorer equity.
Captain and the player Brazil must plan around (64 caps, 11 intl goals). Drifts between lines as Japan's chief creative outlet on the break; Japan's organisation runs through him with Itakura absent.

Ueda is red-hot (brace vs Tunisia, Eredivisie top scorer) and the most likely Japan scorer on the counter. With linchpin Ko Itakura NOT in the XI, Taniguchi anchors a reshuffled back three — a real defensive question mark Brazil will target, but also why Japan may sit deeper and make this a tight, low-event game.

win.reason: Germany's confirmed 4-2-3-1 with Undav (19 BL goals, a goal contribution every ~14 min at this WC) starting as a second striker behind Havertz, plus Wirtz and Sané, gives them the clear edge at home favorite status. But Paraguay's switch from a 5-4-1 to an open 4-3-3 commits Almirón, Enciso and Avalos forward, leaving Enciso real space to run at Rüdiger/Tah on the counter — that genuinely raises draw and over risk, so this is ~55% win probability, not a near-certainty.

scorers.note: Confirmed to start as the second striker/10 with Musiala benched; Bundesliga's top German scorer (19) and already 3 goals at this WC — Germany's sharpest in-form finisher, but Paraguay sit deep so not a lock.
Confirmed lone striker in the 4-2-3-1 and a penalty option (converted vs Curaçao); main aerial/box presence but service must break a packed Paraguay block.
Starts as the creative 10/left of the front three and is on free-kick and corner duty with Raum benched — a scoring and set-piece threat.
Paraguay's confirmed forward in the new 4-3-3 and their best break threat; if isolated against Rüdiger/Tah with space he is their realest goal path, which keeps Paraguay live.

assists.note: Primary chance-creator and set-piece deliverer in the confirmed XI; most likely to set up Undav or Havertz.
Confirmed right-back/deep playmaker and corner taker; his switches and deliveries are a key supply line against a deep block.
Starts wide right; his cutbacks and crosses are a route to assists if he gets at Junior Alonso one-on-one.

penalty.note: Germany pressing a deep, now-stretched 4-3-3 raises spot-kick chances; latest reports list Kimmich as first-choice taker with Havertz next (Havertz scored from the spot vs Curaçao). For Paraguay, Enciso/Almirón would take any pen now Diego Gómez is suspended.
