lineups.away: 4-3-3 confirmed XI: GK Gill; DEF Junior Alonso, Canale, Gustavo Gomez, Caceres; MID Bobadilla, Galarza, Cubas; FWD Almiron, Enciso, Avalos

playerNotes.note: Promoted into the start as the second striker/10; in red-hot form (19 BL goals, 3 at this WC) and now carries Germany's chief goal threat with Musiala benched.
BENCHED — one of Nagelsmann's two changes after the Ecuador defeat. Remains a major late-game impact sub and Germany's most dangerous super-sub to break a tiring block.
Comes in at left-back for David Raum (benched); less established at this level, a side Enciso/Almirón may target on the counter.
Starts up top in place of Sanabria in the new 4-3-3; a 34-year-old target man with a thin scoring record (2 in 25 caps) — more of a hold-up/link reference than a clinical threat.
OUT injured; Canale comes into central defence, a downgrade on aerial/organisation that Havertz and German set-pieces can exploit.

Back from a red-card suspension and a confirmed front-three starter; shares penalty, free-kick and corner duty with Enciso now Diego Gómez is suspended — Paraguay's main delivery in the more open 4-3-3.

win.reason: Netherlands carry more attacking firepower (10 group goals, +6 GD) and Brobbey is in red-hot form, but this is no certainty. The Dutch back line conceded in all three group games, has no clean sheet since November, and pushes full-backs high - exactly the transition space Morocco's counter and Hakimi's pace punish. Morocco sit deep behind a Diop/Riad block. Model ~52% NED / ~20% draw / ~28% MAR; draw+underdog ~48% means this is a lean, not a lock. Extra time is live.

scorers.note: Dutch top scorer with 3 group goals (brace v Sweden, opener v Tunisia); leads the line and finishes inside the box. Powerful hold-up plus runs in behind, but Diop/Riad are a physical CB pairing sitting deep, so not a banker.
Starts wide-left/inside-left and is on Dutch penalty duty, giving him a spot-kick route to a goal on top of open play. Productive group stage; the high left side is where he can isolate Mazraoui.

Morocco's lone 9 and joint top scorer (3 goals incl. fastest of WC2026 v Scotland, plus Brazil and Haiti). Isolated up top against Van Dijk so service is limited, but he scores from minimal chances on the counter - never bank a single striker.
Starts on the right ahead of the benched Summerville; direct runner who can attack Mazraoui's flank in transition. Lower volume than Brobbey/Gakpo, so a value scorer rather than a probable one.

assists.note: Morocco's primary creative outlet from right-back; one of the quickest attacking full-backs in the world, drives the counter and delivers set pieces (corners/free-kicks). Most of Morocco's danger flows through him.

Dutch right-back who bombs forward and supplies crosses for Brobbey; one of the full-backs pushing high. High delivery volume makes him a live assist source, though Morocco's deep block crowds the box.
Carries the ball into the final third from the left and combines with Brobbey; chips in assists alongside his goal threat. Morocco's compact shape can cut off the final pass.

penalty.backup: Achraf Hakimi (Morocco) / Memphis Depay if introduced (Netherlands)
penalty.note: If Morocco win a spot-kick Brahim Diaz is first choice with Hakimi as backup; for the Netherlands Gakpo takes penalties (Depay an option off the bench). Probability medium, not low: Dutch full-backs push high and concede transition fouls, while Morocco defend deep and can give away spot-kicks under pressure.

lineups.home: 4-3-3 confirmed XI: GK Verbruggen; DEF Dumfries, Van Dijk, Van Hecke, Van de Ven; MID Gravenberch, Frenkie de Jong, Reijnders; FWD Malen, Brobbey, Gakpo
lineups.away: 4-2-3-1 confirmed XI: GK Bounou; DEF Hakimi, Diop, Riad, Mazraoui; DM Bouaddi, El Aynaoui; AM Brahim Diaz, Ounahi, El Khannouss; FW Saibari

playerNotes.note: Lone striker with Ayoub El Kaabi benched. Joint tournament-form star: scored in each of his first three WC appearances (Brazil, Scotland - fastest goal of WC2026 at 71s - and Haiti). Isolated against Van Dijk and feeds off counters, so chances scarce but lethal.

Young defensive midfielder forming the double pivot with El Aynaoui in place of the absent Sofyan Amrabat. A less experienced screen in front of the back four - a pressure point if Reijnders and De Jong run at him; key to whether Morocco's deep block holds.

Centre-back pairing (NOT Aguerd/Masina). Physical, deep-sitting duo tasked with containing Brobbey's power and Dutch box runs; their aerial duels and recovery in transition decide whether Morocco keep a clean sheet.
Dutch top scorer (3 goals), starts at striker. Combines raw strength to hold off Diop/Riad with runs in behind. Morocco's deep line gives him less space than open Sweden/Tunisia defences did.

Captain and anchor, played every minute of the group stage, but the back line conceded in all three games with no clean sheet since November. His command in transition is the biggest question against Hakimi's pace and Morocco's break.

Right-back and Morocco's main attacking and set-piece outlet; among the fastest full-backs in the world. The space behind the high-pushing Dutch full-backs is exactly where he is most dangerous, plus penalty/free-kick/corner duty.

scorers.note: 4 goals in 2 WC starts (braces vs Iraq & Senegal); back to full power after the France rest. ~-140 anytime, ~-900 to register a shot on target. Norway's designated penalty taker.
Norway's secondary striker threat — aerial + box presence on the left of the front three. ~+175 anytime.

win.reason: Model: England ~57% (England 57% / draw 25% / Congo DR 18%). Overlapping left-back; cut-backs and crosses into Kane as England overload Congo's right with Rashford.

penalty.note: Sustained box pressure on a deep block over 90' makes a spot-kick a live route — Kane takes it, Bellingham the backup. If Congo win one on the counter it's Wissa (backup Kakuta off the bench) now Bakambu is benched.

win.reason: Confirmed XIs out. USA name their strongest side (identical to the 4-1 Paraguay opener) with Christian Pulisic back to start and Folarin Balogun (brace + MOTM vs Paraguay) leading the line. Bosnia set up 4-4-2 with a deep block, Edin Dzeko (~40) captaining alongside Ermedin Demirovic. Model: United States ~60% (draw ~22% / Bosnia ~18%) — tempered from the pre-lineup number because Bosnia will sit deep and counter, a genuine out-xG draw risk in a knockout. Bosnia mustered only 1.87 xG across three group games, so the personnel gap is wide, but ~60% is not certainty. Most likely 2-1.

scorers.note: 24yo Monaco striker, in form — brace + MOTM vs Paraguay; co-favourite anytime (~+120). Runs the channels vs Bosnia’s static CBs.
Talisman back to start and fit; USA penalty + free-kick + lead-corner taker (13/15 career pens). Co-favourite scorer; stamina the only caveat (77 tournament mins).
Chief creator behind Balogun (assisted his 2nd vs Paraguay); late-arriving goal threat in the box.
Bosnia’s qualifying top scorer and only realistic open-play threat (~+440) — chiefly on the counter or a set piece. NOT captain Dzeko (~40, ~+6500).

assists.note: Marauding LB; overlaps + low cutbacks feeding Pulisic/Balogun down USA’s strongest (left) corridor.
Through-balls between the lines; already set up Balogun in the opener.
Bosnia’s corner/set-piece deliverer — their opener vs Canada came from his corner; the dead ball is Bosnia’s most credible route to a goal.

penalty.note: Attacking a packed low block can win a spot-kick. Pulisic is the undisputed USA taker (13/15 career); if he is subbed for stamina, Tillman steps up. For Bosnia, Esmir Bajraktarevic (scored the winning pen vs Italy in the playoff) would take one.

lineups.home: Matt Freese, Alex Freeman, Chris Richards, Tim Ream, Antonee Robinson, Tyler Adams, Weston McKennie, Sergino Dest, Malik Tillman, Christian Pulisic, Folarin Balogun
lineups.away: Nikola Vasilj, Sead Kolasinac, Tarik Muharemovic, Nikola Katic, Amar Dedic, Kerim Alajbegovic, Ivan Sunjic, Ivan Basic, Esmir Bajraktarevic, Ermedin Demirovic, Edin Dzeko

playerNotes.note: In-form focal striker (brace + MOTM vs Paraguay), ~+120 co-favourite anytime. Pace on the shoulder vs physical-but-static Muharemovic/Katic is the mismatch.
Back to start and fit; USA’s penalty, free-kick and lead-corner taker (13/15 pens). Scorer + assist hub, but on a stamina watch (77 tournament mins, no full 90 since March).
Breakout tournament; the creator behind Balogun (assisted his 2nd vs Paraguay). Also arrives late to finish.
Qualifying top scorer and Bosnia’s realistic goal threat (~+440) — target man / channel runner, dangerous on the counter or a set piece.
21, PSV — Bosnia’s best carrier and transition threat down the right; scored the winning penalty vs Italy in the playoff, so a credible spot-kick taker.
~40, captain — leadership, hold-up and aerial set-piece presence, but ~+6500 anytime. Veteran symbolic starter, NOT a banker goal threat.

sources: Model: bivariate-Poisson xG from ESPN group-stage GF/GA + recency-weighted form

facts: United States: 6 pts, +4 GD across 3 group games (8 for / 4 against).
Bosnia-Herzegovina: 4 pts, -1 GD across 3 group games (5 for / 6 against).
United States's Folarin Balogun leads them with 3 goals in 4 games.
Bosnia-Herzegovina's Ermin Mahmic leads them with 2 goals in 2 games.
assumptions: Model projects United States to carry more of the ball and the better chances (higher attack rating); Bosnia-Herzegovina to sit compact and play on the break.
Goal expectancy 3.0–1.3 (United States–Bosnia-Herzegovina) assumes group-stage scoring rates hold — a big regression risk on a 2–3 game sample.

win.reason: Spain start their strongest XI (Yamal, Oyarzabal, Baena front; Rodri-Pedri-Olmo). Model ~70% Spain / 18% draw / 12% Austria. Austria (Gregoritsch leads line, Arnautovic benched) will sit in a mid/low block and counter — a real draw/low-scoring risk, so win-to-nil and 2+ margin lines are downgraded. Draw-no-bet is the safer route.

scorers.note: Spain's form striker + confirmed penalty taker; 2 goals in the group. Cleanest Spain anytime pick from the confirmed XI.
On the mark this tournament and Spain's chief threat — but Rangnick's plan is explicitly to double-team him, capping the anytime value.
Scored the 1-0 winner vs Uruguay and now carries the width + set-piece load with Williams/Pino injured. In form.
Starts up front with Arnautovic benched, but he's a link/target man who assists more than he finishes; low anytime chance vs Spain's 0-conceded group.

assists.note: On free kicks, corners and open-play creation with Williams out — Spain's primary supply line tonight.
Volume creator from the right; assisted twice in the group stage.
Austria's dead-ball delivery onto Danso/Posch — their most credible set-piece assist route.

penalty.note: Spain's Oyarzabal takes spot-kicks; Yamal the backup. Yamal/Baena dribbling draws box fouls. For Austria, with Arnautovic benched the penalty duty shifts to Marcel Sabitzer.

win.reason: Confirmed XIs in. Portugal ~56% (Opta 56.2 / draw 24 / Croatia 19.5) — NOT a lock, ~44% for draw-or-Croatia. Leão + João Neves start (Félix, Rúben Neves benched): more pace and legs. But Croatia will sit deep, slow tempo through Modrić–Kovačić and threaten set-pieces — a genuine draw / low-scoring trap.

scorers.note: Striker + primary penalty taker, but 41 and feast-or-famine: brace vs Uzbekistan then BLANKED vs Colombia. Non-banker anytime scorer vs a deep, physical, dribble-defending block.
The matchup edge — isolated 1v1 vs 40-yr-old Perišić with Mendes overlapping. High shot/goal-involvement expectation on the left.
Arriving runs, set-piece routines, penalty backup — multiple ways to score. Highest chance volume in the side.
Croatia lone striker + set-piece focal point; scored the 1-0 winner vs Panama. Their likeliest scorer if they nick one.

assists.note: Chief creator and primary set-piece deliverer (corners/free-kicks).
Overlapping LB feeding Leão, plus direct free-kicks — doubled-up left side vs Perišić.
Croatia creator + corner/FK deliverer (assisted Vlašić winner vs Ghana); source of any Croatia set-piece goal.

penalty.note: Ronaldo stays primary penalty taker for his final World Cup, Bruno Fernandes co/backup. Croatia sit deep so a spot-kick is less likely; Modrić the senior taker if Croatia win one.

But Australia (40.7% possession, only 2 conceded, 0-0 v Paraguay) are built to frustrate and counter through Irankunda, so this is genuinely close to a draw/underdog scenario, not a certainty.

scorers.note: Egypt's sole penalty taker and focal point; 3 WC goals already and all-time Egypt WC top scorer, but a packed Australian back-five limits his space cutting in from the right.
Man City forward starts central; pace and direct running are the best route to break a low block, but service into a compact box is the question.
Starts wide-right in the front three; secondary runner who can arrive late if Australia collapse onto Salah and Marmoush.
Lone striker and Australia's counter x-factor; lightning pace and a powerful strike make him the outlet on the break, but he'll be isolated for long spells.

assists.note: Already has 2 assists this WC (Ashour v Belgium, Marmoush link-up); Egypt's chief creator whether he scores or supplies.
Central link man in the midfield three tasked with threading runners into the box against the deep block.
Direct runner who can pull defenders and lay off for Salah or arriving midfielders.

penalty.note: Salah is Egypt's confirmed sole penalty taker. A packed Australian box defending a low block raises the chance of a spot-kick if Egypt sustain pressure; Marmoush is the natural backup if Salah is off the pitch.

Man City forward now starts as the central striker after Egypt's shift to 4-3-3. Pace, directness and clinical finishing (11 goals in 49 Egypt caps); his running in behind is Egypt's best tool to stretch a compact defence.

20-year-old x-factor deployed as the lone forward. Lightning quick with a powerful strike; Australia's youngest-ever WC scorer (v Turkey). Isolated in the low block but the primary counter outlet when Australia win the ball.
Breakout Feyenoord full-back operating as right wing-back in the back five. Must handle the Salah/Marmoush overload on Egypt's attacking flank while providing width when Australia break.
35-year-old, ninth most-capped Socceroo, at left wing-back. Experience is key to keeping Egypt's right-side combinations quiet and marshalling the low block on that side.
Advanced midfielder in the three, Egypt's chief link between midfield and the front line. Scored v Belgium (Salah assist); tasked with unlocking Australia's block with runners into the box.

win.reason: Confirmed strongest XI: Messi + Lautaro start (both rested for the Jordan dead rubber), Mac Allister, Enzo, De Paul, Romero all back. Model still ~72% (Argentina 72% / draw 20% / Cape Verde 8%), but Cape Verde name a deep 4-1-4-1 built to defend — the same block that held Spain 0-0. Argentina lean, not a lock; the draw/Under-2.5 tail is live.

scorers.note: 6 goals so far (~75% of Argentina's output), scoring in 7 straight WC games — but 39, 0 assists, and about to be double-teamed by a designed low block. Prime scorer, NOT a banker.

Captain, 22 international goals (squad-best), wide-right pace — Cape Verde's only realistic scorer, and only on a counter or set-piece.

assists.note: Tournament passing leader (177 passes) — the metronome most likely to thread the ball through a compact defence.
Beats his man 1-v-1 to pull the low block open and cut it back — natural assist source against a deep line.
Back in after a rest; carries and links, feeds Messi in the pockets between the CV lines.

penalty.note: Favourites probing a defend-deep side that concedes box fouls — a penalty is a real possibility. Messi takes it, Lautaro is next in line (Julián Álvarez off the bench after that).

win.reason: Colombia are clear possession-favourites with James, Díaz and Córdoba, but Ghana under Queiroz sit in a disciplined low block (held England 0-0) and counter through Semenyo and Iñaki Williams. Colombia laboured to a 1-0 over DR Congo against exactly this kind of block, so a win is likely but not certain; draw + Ghana upset sits around 46-49%.

scorers.note: Peak-form Bayern winger (26 G / 19 A across the club season); pace stretches a deep block and gets him into high-value positions, also a converted-penalty option — Colombia's most dangerous starter but not a banker vs a packed box.

Russian Premier League top scorer (17 goals) and league Footballer of the Year; physical central striker who is Colombia's on-pitch focal point and a threat on crosses/second balls against Ghana's back four.

Captain and primary penalty taker; left-footed set-piece deliverer who assisted from a corner in the Copa América. Chance creator first, but arrives late and strikes from the edge — real scorer/pen equity, not a banker at 34.
Lone striker spearheading Ghana's counter; his raw pace in behind is the Black Stars' clearest route to a goal if they can spring transitions off Colombia's high line.

assists.note: Colombia's chief creator and set-piece deliverer (corners + left-footed free-kicks); most of Colombia's clear chances route through him.
Overlapping right-back who scored vs DR Congo and provides width and cut-backs — a key supply line to break a low block from the flank.
Explosive on the counter (fitness permitting after the ankle knock); Ghana's most likely creator for Williams on transitions and the biggest single threat to Colombia's clean sheet.

penalty.note: James is Colombia's first-call penalty taker; Díaz has also converted (vs Costa Rica) and is the backup. For Ghana, Jordan Ayew is the designated taker with a perfect career record (17/17) if they win one on the counter.

lineups.away: Lawrence Ati Zigi, Marvin Senaya, Jerome Opoku, Derrick Luckassen, Gideon Mensah, Thomas Partey, Caleb Yirenkyi, Kwasi Sibo, Jordan Ayew, Antoine Semenyo, Iñaki Williams

playerNotes.note: The single lever of Ghana's game plan — shields the back four in front of the two centre-backs and breaks up Colombia's central combinations. If he screens well, Colombia are forced wide and the low block holds.

Key to any Ghana clean-sheet / draw outcome; this Ghana side held England 0-0 behind a deep block, so a busy Ati Zigi keeping it level is a live scenario — one reason to downgrade Colombia win-to-nil.

Listed questionable with an ankle knock but in the XI; his explosiveness is Ghana's biggest transition threat. Fit-and-firing Semenyo raises Ghana's ceiling sharply; if muted, their attacking output collapses.
Colombia's game-breaker in career-best form; against a packed box his ability to beat a man 1v1 and get shots off is the most probable source of the opening goal.
Captain, primary pen taker and set-piece deliverer; at 34 he is the creative hub but must be treated as a creator, not a banker scorer, against a compact defence.
Attacking right-back who scored the DR Congo winner; his overlaps and cut-backs are a primary tool to unlock Ghana's low block from wide areas.

win.reason: On the confirmed teamsheets Morocco keep their unchanged, deeper spine — Hakimi and Mazraoui overlapping, a Bouaddi/El Aynaoui double pivot behind a Díaz/Ounahi/El Khannouss trio and in-form Saibari (3 group goals) leading the line. Canada lose scoring pop with Larin, Choinière and Cornelius benched and Koné out, leaning on David and an untested Oluwaseyi with Bombito reshuffled into central defence. Morocco are the higher-seeded, unbeaten side who counter through Hakimi, but Canada's high press keeps this a genuine ~50% game — the draw and a Canada upset both hold real weight.

scorers.note: Now leads the line with El Kaabi benched; scored in all three group games plus the shootout winner vs Netherlands — Morocco's hottest threat but not a banker.
Canada's talisman and penalty taker; hat-trick vs Qatar. With Larin benched he is the clear focal point, but Morocco defend deep and well — non-banker.
Advanced No.10 in the trio and Morocco's first-choice in-game penalty taker; a live goal route if a spot-kick is awarded.
Starts centrally with Larin benched but is yet to score this tournament despite heavy minutes — a transition threat, not reliable.

assists.note: Morocco's chief creator from the overlapping right; box touches leader vs Netherlands and backup set-piece taker to Díaz.
Controlled midfield vs Netherlands; the line-breaking passer feeding Saibari from the attacking trio.
Canada's most consistent attacking fullback down the right; primary crossing outlet with Ahmed/Buchanan ahead.

penalty.note: Díaz is Morocco's confirmed first-choice in-game penalty taker (Hakimi next in line); for Canada, Jonathan David is the designated taker. Two attack-minded, overlapping-fullback sides plus Canada's press make a spot-kick realistic but not high-probability.

win.reason: France clearly stronger and confirmed near-full strength (Mbappé 6 goals, Dembélé 4, Olise 5 assists). BUT Paraguay's CONFIRMED XI is a deep 5-3-2 block — Ávalos benched, Almirón + Enciso kept for the counter — the exact model that dumped Germany out on penalties (1-1, Gill saved two in the shootout). This is a genuine low-scoring / draw-risk night, not a formality: France ~60-63%, draw ~22%, Paraguay ~15%. Back France to win but respect draw-no-bet, under-2.5 and a France-only-by-one; do NOT overpay their win-to-nil/correct-score.

scorers.note: 6 goals in 4 games, level for the Golden Boot and in career-best knockout form; stretches Paraguay's five-man line and is on penalties.
4 goals incl. a hat-trick vs Norway; France's right-side threat, isolates the Paraguay left wing-back.
2 goals; pace down the left with Digne overlapping — the channel outside Cáceres is the space to hit.
Scored Paraguay's header vs Germany and their main transition outlet — Paraguay's realistic route to a goal is an Enciso/Almirón counter.

assists.note: 5 assists in the group stage; France's free-kick + corner taker and creative hub against a packed box.
2 assists; drops to link when defences sit deep and feeds Dembélé/Barcola.
Starts ahead of Ávalos; drifts into the No.10 space and carries Paraguay's counter alongside Enciso.

penalty.note: Against a deep five-man block defending crosses, a France penalty or set-piece is a live route to the opener — Mbappé takes them, Dembélé next. Paraguay's own order runs Enciso, G. Gómez, Almirón.

win.reason: Brazil are the deserved favourites (-130 market) with the tournament's form winger in Vinicius Jr (4 goals, 1 assist, tournament-leading 3.87 xG) plus Cunha and Martinelli fluid up top, Casemiro (goal + MOTM vs Japan) and Bruno Guimaraes (4 WC assists) controlling midfield. But Norway defend deep, counter with Haaland (5 WC goals, scored in 13 straight internationals) and are historically unbeaten vs Brazil in 4 meetings (2W 2D). ~58-62% true win prob, so the draw + Norway smash-and-grab carry ~38-42% — this is not a formality.

scorers.note: Tournament top scorer for Brazil (4 goals, 1 assist), most xG of any player (3.87), 2nd-most shots on target. Direct duel down the left against makeshift RB Marcus Holmgren Pedersen (in for injured Ryerson) is Brazil's clearest edge. Likely on-pitch penalty option with Paqueta out and Raphinha benched. Not a lock though — Norway sit deep and double him.

5 WC goals, scored in his last 13 internationals, netted the 86th-min winner vs Ivory Coast. Norway's whole plan is service to him on the counter and from Odegaard set pieces. But he can blank against Marquinhos + Gabriel Magalhaes on limited supply — explicitly NOT a banker.
3 group-stage goals incl. a brace vs Haiti; starts as the false-9 who drops and interchanges with Vini and Martinelli, gets into the box late. Genuine goal threat from open play.

Physical aerial foil to Haaland, arrived in spectacular form. Norway's set-piece/aerial threat (Sorloth + Haaland + Ajer) against Brazil is real — a header from an Odegaard delivery is a live route to a Norway goal.

assists.note: Leads Brazil with 4 assists at this World Cup; the deep-lying creator threading balls for Vini and Cunha to break the low block.

Takes Norway's corners and free-kicks and is their chief creator; against a deep block his set-piece deliveries to Haaland/Sorloth/Ajer are Norway's most likely assist source. Fitness in the knockouts is the swing factor.
Not just a scorer — his beating of Pedersen 1v1 to pull back for Cunha/Martinelli is a prime creator route. Also had an assist in the group stage.

penalty.note: With Paqueta OUT (hamstring) and Raphinha on the bench, senior on-pitch duty most likely falls to Vinicius Junior; Cunha is a listed set-piece taker as backup. For Norway a penalty would go to Haaland, with Odegaard also an option. Deep-block matches with a set-piece-heavy underdog carry a moderate spot-kick chance either way.

lineups.away: Orjan Nyland, Marcus Holmgren Pedersen, Kristoffer Ajer, Torbjorn Heggem, David Moller Wolfe, Sander Berge, Patrick Berg, Martin Odegaard, Antonio Nusa, Erling Haaland, Alexander Sorloth

Was an adductor doubt but confirmed to START; scored and was MOTM vs Japan. Screens the back four and is Brazil's key insurance against Norway's Haaland-led counters.
5 WC goals and scored in his last 13 internationals; the entire Norway gameplan funnels to him on the break and from Odegaard set pieces. Solbakken manages his minutes, but he starts here.
Norway's creator and set-piece taker (corners + free-kicks). His delivery to Haaland/Sorloth/Ajer is Norway's clearest scoring route; his knockout fitness swings how competitive Norway are.
Direct, hard-to-contain RW who curled in the winner-setup goal vs Ivory Coast; his 1v1 running down the right at Douglas Santos is Norway's transition outlet and a defensive test for Brazil's LB.

win.reason: Model rebased on the CONFIRMED XIs: Mexico 50% / draw 29% / England 21%. Mexico still shade it — unbeaten, zero goals conceded in four games, home crowd and 2,240m altitude at the Azteca — but England's confirmed XI is materially stronger than the one the model priced (Saka and Gordon start over Madueke and Rashford), so the 53% baseline is trimmed. Draw + England = 50%: this is a lean, not a lock.

scorers.note: 3 goals in 4 games including the tournament opener and a top-corner strike vs Ecuador; starts LW where England's debutant RB Quansah defends.
5 goals in 4 games, England's all-time World Cup record scorer (11+), penalty taker — and his supply line improves with Saka + Gordon confirmed either side of Bellingham.
2 goals in 4 games, Mexico's designated penalty taker (93% career conversion on 30 attempts) and shares free-kick duty.
Confirmed starter after managed minutes (Achilles) — assisted off the bench vs Croatia; England's secondary penalty option behind Kane.
17-year-old starts again in the free-role that unpicked Ecuador's line — arrives late in the box; longshot with real live-ball involvement.

assists.note: 3 assists this tournament and Mexico's corner taker — starts on the right, directly at Quansah on his first World Cup start.
2 assists off the bench vs DR Congo, now CONFIRMED to start on the left — England's most direct creator in this XI.
Assisted Rashford's clincher vs Croatia in an 18-minute cameo; shares corner duty with Rice and starts RW feeding Kane.

penalty.note: Mexico's taker is Jiménez — 93.3% conversion on 30 career penalties. England's taker is Harry Kane (Saka backup). Both boxes now carry direct dribblers (Quiñones/Alvarado vs Saka/Gordon), so a spot-kick either way is livelier than the group-stage read suggested.

3 goals in 4 including the tournament opener; scored again vs Ecuador. Runs at England's most inexperienced defensive channel.
2 goals this tournament, Mexico's penalty taker (93% career conversion) and the focal point who brings Mora/Alvarado into play at his fourth World Cup.

win.reason: Confirmed XIs are out. Spain (4-3-3: Simon; Porro, Cubarsi, Laporte, Cucurella; Rodri, Pedri; Yamal, Olmo, Baena; Oyarzabal) are unbeaten with ZERO goals conceded all tournament and Rodri fit - the model favourite at ~44% (Portugal ~28% / draw ~28%). Portugal swap in Joao Felix for Rafael Leao, trading transition pace for link-play, so their counter is a shade less lethal. But ~44% is not certainty: a ~28% draw plus extra-time/pens is very much alive, and possession-Spain vs a countering Portugal is a real out-xG trap. Lean Spain, respect the draw.

scorers.note: Spain's spearhead and penalty taker - tournament-leading 4 goals (braces v Saudi & Austria) plus an assist. In form and on spot-kicks; the strongest single scorer on the card.

Confirmed to start and on penalties/free-kicks, 3 goals this WC - but at 41 he is a low-touch poacher, not a banker; against a Spain side yet to concede, his cleanest route is a set-piece or a spot-kick.
Starts on the right off 141 group minutes back from a hamstring but looked sharp v Austria; his duel with Nuno Mendes is a game-swinger and he carries a real anytime threat cutting inside.
Between-the-lines creator arriving late into the box behind Oyarzabal; Spain's second-line runner and a live anytime option if the game opens.

assists.note: Spain's primary free-kick and corner taker plus open-play creator from the left - highest set-piece volume in the XI, their best assist route against a packed box.
Right-wing chance creator; his deliveries and cut-backs for Oyarzabal are Spain's sharpest open-play supply.
Portugal's creative hub and corner taker, backup penalty/FK man - their likeliest provider, especially from the set-piece deliveries that are Portugal's best goal path here.

penalty.note: An in-game spot-kick is a medium shout, but with four clean sheets between them and a tight knockout the bigger call is a SHOOTOUT - genuinely live at ~23%. Oyarzabal (Spain) and Ronaldo (Portugal) are the confirmed primary takers.

lineups.away: Unai Simon, Pedro Porro, Pau Cubarsi, Aymeric Laporte, Marc Cucurella, Rodri, Pedri, Lamine Yamal, Dani Olmo, Alex Baena, Mikel Oyarzabal

playerNotes.note: THE lineup change - starts on the left for Rafael Leao. A tactical call by Martinez (pressing/link-play over Leao's direct pace), which makes Portugal's left a touch less transition-lethal; Leao now a pace weapon off the bench late.
Confirmed to start up top and on penalties; tournament-leading 4 goals plus an assist - Spain's clearest match-winner.
Starts and fit despite a bloody knee v Croatia; 3 WC goals and primary pen/FK taker - but 41 and a low-touch poacher, so a non-banker against a Spain side yet to concede.
Fit and starting at the base of midfield - Spain are 'a different team' with him; his control vs Neves/Vitinha decides the possession share.
Starts right off limited minutes (hamstring-managed) but sharp v Austria; his duel with Nuno Mendes is the marquee matchup.
Elite 1v1 defender tasked with Yamal while also joining Portugal's attacking left with Felix - the hinge of both boxes.

win.reason: USA name an unchanged, cohesive XI for the third straight match with Balogun restored to the exact system Pochettino built around him, pressing high through the Adams-McKennie double pivot and transitioning via Pulisic and Tillman. Belgium arrive off a draining extra-time slog with Senegal and field a misfiring De Ketelaere false-nine (Lukaku benched), leaning on De Bruyne's dead-ball delivery and Doku/Trossard counters. Home crowd in Seattle plus fresher legs give USA a genuine edge, but Belgium's counter and KDB's set-pieces keep this a real coin-flip.

scorers.note: Leads USA with 3 group-stage goals (incl. a double vs Paraguay) and starts through the middle; red card overturned so he is available. Faces a Mechele/Theate pairing that can be run in behind, but Courtois lowers conversion.
USA's most-feared attacker from the left, matched against RB Castagne and able to cut inside. A primary shot and set-piece-adjacent threat.
Buried a curling free-kick vs Bosnia in the R32 and works between the lines as the No.10; late box runs plus a set-piece option.
Starts left of Belgium's front three, one of their two most dangerous counter outlets at Freeman/Dest's side. Belgium's toothless attack caps his edge.

assists.note: Belgium's creative engine and primary set-piece/free-kick deliverer; most chance creation runs through his dead-ball service and through-balls to Doku/Trossard.
Chief chance-creator from the left for USA; fed Balogun's opener vs Paraguay and drives the transition attack.
Overlapping LB providing width and cutbacks behind Pulisic; a repeat crossing outlet when USA push Belgium's tired back line deep.

penalty.note: De Bruyne is Belgium's designated first penalty taker and set-piece specialist; Tielemans converted the extra-time winner vs Senegal and is the clear backup. USA's likely taker is Pulisic. Transition-heavy game raises box contact but neither side draws penalties at a high rate.

playerNotes.note: Anchors the double pivot with McKennie; his ball-winning is the mechanism to disrupt De Bruyne and spring USA's transitions — the key duel is Adams vs KDB.
Box-to-box CM alongside Adams; late runs into the area and must track Tielemans/Vanaken to stop Belgium's midfield overloads.
Right-wing dribble threat and Belgium's main counter outlet; subbed at 56' vs Senegal (fatigue). Direct test for LB Antonee Robinson.
Starts as a misfiring false-nine with Lukaku on the bench; Belgium look toothless in this shape, underwriting the low-scoring/draw risk.
Attacking RB who joins the press and overlaps Freeman; exposed if Trossard isolates him — a two-way swing factor.
Elite keeper and Belgium's biggest edge — his shot-stopping is why USA's correct-score and win-to-nil lines should be shaded longer despite USA creating the better chances.

win.reason: Confirmed-XI read: Argentina 62% / draw 24% / Egypt 14%. Scaloni beefed the midfield (Paredes anchor in, Almada out) and picked Álvarez's press over Lautaro — built to break a deep block. But Egypt's confirmed 4-2-3-1 low block (Marmoush benched, extra legs in Lashin/Hassan) is a genuine draw-and-under profile: under 2.5 has hit in 5 of Egypt's last 6, and market consensus is Argentina + under.

scorers.note: Scored in 8 straight WC matches, Golden Boot leader (6 group goals), takes pens + FKs. Still NOT a banker: he's 39, played 120 min Friday, and Egypt's block starves the box — veteran stars are non-banker by rule.
Confirmed starter over Lautaro for his pressing (28.6 pressing actions/90). 0 G/A this tournament — picked for work rate, but he's the man on the end of the front-two moves.
Egypt's whole attack with Marmoush benched: goal+assist vs NZ, Panenka in the R32 shootout, sole pen/set-piece taker. Counters at Tagliafico's flank are Egypt's one road to goal.
Arrives late from midfield into the space a deep block concedes at the edge of the box; second-ball threat on Messi/Paredes corners.

assists.note: Everything runs through him vs a low block — corners, free kicks, and the final pass between the lines. Creator is the safer Messi market than scorer today.
Right-side link man and a corner taker in the confirmed XI; feeds Messi's half-space.

win.reason: Confirmed XIs change the read. France name their full attack — Mbappé, Dembélé, Olise, Doué (Barcola benched). Morocco field NO recognised striker (Saibari out injured, Rahimi benched, Halhal & Riad unavailable): Talbi + Anass Salah-Eddine in, a false-nine front of Díaz/Ounahi/El Khannouss with Hakimi overlapping. That's a deep-block-and-counter setup, so this leans France ~60% / draw ~24% / Morocco ~16%. France should make the running; the 2022 semi (France 2-0 on fewer touches) is the template — but a strikerless low block is a genuine draw/under trap.

3 WC assists from RB and Morocco's main set-piece deliverer — the overlap/cut-back that set up Ounahi vs Canada is their primary route.

penalty.note: Cagey knockout vs a deep block invites box contact, so a spot-kick is live. France's is settled: Mbappé (scored the winner vs Paraguay), Dembélé backup. Morocco's is UNSETTLED post-Saibari — Hakimi is senior but missed his last shootout pen vs the Netherlands, so treat their taker (Hakimi/Díaz) as a doubt.

win.reason: Confirmed XIs firm up two things. Guehi passed his fitness test and starts, so the Stones-Guehi-Konsa spine England want against Haaland stays intact — the 'defensive chaos' angle is gone. But Ezri Konsa, a centre-back, is deputising at right-back for the suspended Quansah: far less overlap, England's width funnels left onto Gordon, and that seam is exactly where Norway's Wolfe-Schjelderup left side will attack. Norway sit deep and counter through Odegaard->Haaland (Schjelderup, who set up both Haaland goals vs Brazil, now starts over Nusa). Genuine ~55% England lean, not a formality — draw-and-Norway equity together is close to 45%, so the price stays honest and the win-to-nil lines get downgraded.

win.reason: Argentina are reigning champions and clear favourites (Opta ~57% to win in 90, bet365 -270 to advance), but the knockout run has been shaky — they conceded in both KO games and needed late comebacks vs Cape Verde (ET) and Egypt (from 2-0 down). Switzerland reach the QF for the first time since 1954 by defending deep and low, drawing 0-0 with Colombia before winning on penalties; Xhaka anchors a compact block built to frustrate a possession side. This is a genuine draw/upset risk (~45% between a Swiss win/draw), so back Argentina but do not treat it as a formality.

resolution.shootout: Slight edge Argentina (E. Martinez pedigree), but Switzerland just beat Colombia on pens — genuinely tight.
resolution.note: Argentina favoured to win the 90 (60%), yet a Swiss deep block + shootout record push extra time (22%) and penalties (18%) into play.

scorers.note: Tournament top-scorer (8), 8-game scoring streak — but 39 and vs a deep block; NOT a banker at his age

assists.note: Primary creator; the deep block invites him to supply rather than finish

penalty.note: Messi is Argentina's designated taker; Lautaro converted vs Jordan when Messi was rested and is the backup. Against a deep block, spot-kicks/handballs are a real route to the opener.
